This matters for any scientific result where the model is part of the claim — climate ensemble runs, drug-discovery surrogates, ML-accelerated materials science.
Caveat. Determinism does not make a simulation correct, only repeatable. A bug in either the model or the sampler is reproduced exactly the same way every time. The benefit is that the bug is debuggable; the benefit is not that there are no bugs.


Debugging at the architectural level
Anyone who has chased a non-determinism bug across GPUs knows the cost. A reproducible substrate does not make ML easier, but it makes the bugs you do hit actually debuggable, because failures are the same every time. ABNG’s cjcl abng inspect --node N --audit filters the log to a single node; descend_traced captures routing paths for individual predictions; abng_predict_snap captures prediction-time state. These don’t automate diagnosis but they make manual diagnosis tractable.
Caveat. The audit log is a postmortem artefact, not a live debugging surface. Stepping through a 10⁶-event chain to find which observation corrupted which posterior is a real human-time cost. Most users will treat the log as a “in case of emergency, break glass” tool rather than a daily-driver debugger.

title: Deterministic and Auditable Neural Systems
text:
What this approach cannot do
The determinism contract has fundamental costs:

No GPU acceleration
Covered above. The determinism contract is incompatible with the vendor BLAS / cuDNN / FlashAttention reduction orderings that make modern accelerator hardware fast. A GPU-equipped workstation gets no measurable speedup over a laptop for ABNG training. This is fundamental — recovery would require giving up bit-identical replay.


No multi-thread training within one graph
The multi-graph arena is per-thread (multiple graphs can coexist), but a single graph’s training loop is single-threaded by design. decide_step’s deterministic node iteration order matters; parallelizing it would require careful synchronization and would probably break the byte-identical-replay property. Phase 0.8 is considering per-leaf parallelism for independent observations, but contingent on profiling.


No live debugging beyond inspection tooling
The audit log is read-only at runtime — you can cjcl abng inspect it, but you can’t gdb it. For users who need step-through debugging of model state evolution, the audit chain is a replacement for that capability (replay any past state) rather than an interactive tool.


The audit log is large
Even with witness-only events at ~80 bytes apiece, four state updates per observation across 1 M training rows is ~320 MB of audit log. Without compaction this is unmanageable in memory and expensive to keep in active storage. The StatsSnapshot marker + smart-replay path addresses this but at the cost of forward-only audit walk (you can’t quickly inspect arbitrary past state without re-replaying from a checkpoint).

title: Deterministic and Auditable Neural Systems
section: What would convince me to deploy this
text:
What would convince me to deploy this
The “git commit history for a model” pitch is compelling in theory. Before deploying ABNG in a real regulated-ML setting, four things would need to happen:

Cross-platform CI verification. The locked SHA-256 canary hashes are demonstrated on x86-64 Linux, macOS, and Windows in CJC-Lang’s test suite. ARM and BSD are untested. A GitHub Actions matrix running the full ABNG test suite on Linux+ARM, macOS+ARM, and Windows+x64, with canary comparison, is a finite engineering project — a cross-platform-determinism.yml workflow skeleton exists in the repo (Phase 0.6), but verification results have not been published. Until they are, “bit-identical determinism” carries an implicit footnote.
Wall-clock cost characterization at production scale. Part 3 measured at n=1k, 10k, 100k rows. Real regulated-ML datasets are often 10⁶–10⁹ rows. Extrapolated from the 100K-row numbers: a 10⁶-row training history would produce a ~300 MB audit log with ~16-second replay. A 10⁹-row run would produce a ~300 GB log with ~16,000-second replay — not impossible but not casually verifiable. Realistic deployment would require log compaction tuned to the workload, and Phase 0.8 is the natural milestone for that.
A real-world case study. A practitioner using ABNG for a problem with real stakes — a regulated trial, a scientific replication package, a robotic-system audit — and writing up what worked, what didn’t, and what they would do differently. Until this exists, ABNG is software without a user. The closest existing thing is the synthetic clinical-trial scenario walked through above, which is a useful proof-of-concept but not a deployment.
An adversarial security review. The “tamper-evident, not tamper-proof” framing is correct, but a serious deployment would benefit from independent security review of the audit-chain construction. Specifically: is SHA-256 the right hash function for this application’s expected lifetime (collision resistance vs preimage resistance vs both)? Are there any non-obvious replay attacks on the chain structure? Does the smart-replay path have any subtle invariants a malicious snapshot could violate? These questions would take a real security team a few weeks to answer competently.







The honest summary



ABNG’s audit chain mechanism is correct and well-tested at the engineering level. The Phase 3 benchmarks confirm it scales linearly to 100K rows; the lineage attestation demo proves the three-signal spoof detection works at 64-row scale. The mechanism itself is well-understood cryptographically — SHA-256 hash chains and Merkle structures have a substantial security literature.
The gap between this and “production-ready for regulated ML” is not theoretical, it’s operational: cross-platform verification, scale testing, real deployment, security review. Those are months of work, not days, and the cost-benefit decision for any given organization depends on whether their use case actually needs Level 3 auditability or whether MLflow / DVC’s Level 1 is sufficient.
For most ML, Level 1 is enough. For the subset where it isn’t, ABNG is the most rigorous experimental answer the open-source ecosystem currently has — and it is unambiguously an experimental answer, not a deployment-ready one.


If after reading this article you decide the audit-chain approach is interesting but probably wrong in three specific ways, this piece has done its job. The architecture itself is in Part 1; the empirical backbone is in Part 3. The open-source code is at crates/cjc-abng/ — bring your skepticism.






Note



ABNG lives at crates/cjc-abng/ in the CJC-Lang repository. The full codebase, the test suite (including the three-signal spoof detection demo at tests/test_abng_lineage_attestation.rs), and the working cjcl abng {inspect, replay, diff, explain, train} CLI surface are open source. Part 1 of this series covers the architecture; Part 3 covers the empirical evidence. This article was the audit and determinism story — the part of the pitch that justifies the determinism premium.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text: 🕑 ~32 min read

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
What is ABNG?
ABNG — Adaptive Belief Network Graph — is a cjc-abng crate that lives alongside the existing CJC-Lang workspace. The goal: provide ML primitives that go beyond “fit some weights to data” by adding three orthogonal axes:

Bayesian uncertainty at every prediction (mean, epistemic leverage, aleatoric variance) — without MCMC, ensembles, or variational inference.
Cryptographic provenance of training history — every observation, every weight update, every structural decision lands in a SHA-256 audit chain that detects post-hoc tampering.
Adaptive structure — the graph’s topology genuinely changes under the workload. Nodes grow, split, merge, prune, compress, freeze under a deterministic decision-policy engine.

The architecture is a radix-style graph: a root node descends into children indexed by prefix bytes from a quantile codebook, and each leaf carries a Bayesian Linear Regression posterior over a feature basis. Predictions descend the tree, the posterior at the resolved leaf gives (mean, epistemic_leverage, aleatoric_var), and structural decisions fire periodically to adapt the tree.




flowchart LR
    F["State<br/>features"] --> CB["Codebook<br/>encode"]
    CB --> P["Prefix<br/>bytes"]
    P --> D["Descend<br/>tree"]
    D --> L["Leaf<br/>BLR"]
    L --> Pred["(mean,<br/>leverage,<br/>aleatoric)"]
    L -.->|every step| AC["Audit chain<br/>SHA-256"]


ABNG conceptual flow. State features → codebook prefix → descend tree → BLR posterior at leaf → (mean, leverage, aleatoric) — plus an audit chain that hashes every step.



It’s exposed to CJC-Lang as 75 abng_* builtins — abng_new, abng_set_codebook, abng_set_leaf_head, abng_set_blr_prior, abng_observe, abng_blr_predict, abng_descend, abng_decide_step, abng_stamp_provenance, etc. — so you can build and drive an ABNG graph entirely from .cjcl source.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
The proof discipline
Every capability in this post has the same proof structure:

A workload — a small but non-trivial problem where the capability should manifest if it’s working.
A specific assertion that would fail if the capability didn’t work.
A locked SHA-256 canary of the resulting graph’s chain_head, gating any future regression.
Cross-pipeline determinism: most demos run through both cjc-eval (AST tree-walk) AND cjc-mir-exec (MIR register-machine) with byte-identical printed output asserted.

That last property is the strongest determinism gate the project supports. AST and MIR are completely different code paths — if any FP arithmetic, hash computation, or dispatch routing differs anywhere in the pipeline, the printed output diverges and the parity test fails.
OK — let’s walk through what’s actually proven.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 1 — Per-state Bayesian uncertainty
Demo: PINN heat-equation solver (tests/test_abng_pinn_uncertainty_cjcl.rs + pinn_source.rs)

What this demo proves
The Bayesian Linear Regression posterior at each codebook-routed leaf returns a 3-tuple (mean, epistemic_leverage, aleatoric_var). The headline assertion is a strict spatial-partition property:
min(epistemic_leverage at edge probes) > max(epistemic_leverage at interior probes)
This is stronger than “average edge uncertainty exceeds average interior uncertainty” — it’s a no-overlap claim. After training, the worst-case interior point has lower uncertainty than the best-case edge point. The signal cleanly partitions evidence-rich vs evidence-poor regions of the input space.
The fit-quality assertion (abs_err_50 < 0.05) proves the mean also converges to the analytical solution. Without this, “leverage is uncertain at edges” would be vacuous — the mean could just be wrong everywhere.


The math
ABNG’s BLR uses a Normal-Inverse-Gamma (NIG) conjugate prior on (w, σ²). Given a prior (μ₀, Λ₀, a₀, b₀) and a stream of observations D = {(φᵢ, yᵢ)}, the conjugate update yields a closed-form posterior:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Lambda_n &= \Lambda_0 + \Phi^\top \Phi \\
\mu_n &= \Lambda_n^{-1}\,(\Lambda_0 \mu_0 + \Phi^\top y) \\
a_n &= a_0 + n/2 \\
b_n &= b_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(y - \Phi\mu_n)^\top(y - \Phi\mu_n) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\mu_0 - \mu_n)^\top \Lambda_0\,(\mu_0 - \mu_n)
\end{aligned}
\]
For a query point x*, the predictive distribution p(y* | x*, D) is a Student-t. ABNG returns its three sufficient ingredients as:








Output
Formula
Interpretation




mean
\(\mu_n^\top \varphi(x^*)\)
Posterior mean prediction (point estimate)


epistemic_leverage
\(\varphi(x^*)^\top \Lambda_n^{-1}\,\varphi(x^*)\)
Variance contribution from posterior over w (model uncertainty)


aleatoric_var
\(b_n / (a_n - 1)\)
Observation-noise variance (data uncertainty)



Two cleanly separated uncertainty channels. epistemic_leverage shrinks as ΦᵀΦ accumulates — more data → tighter posterior over w → less model uncertainty. aleatoric_var converges to the irreducible noise floor. A vanilla MLP collapses both into a single point estimate; ABNG keeps them factored.


Why the heat equation
The 1-D heat equation ∂u/∂t = α∂²u/∂x² with Dirichlet BCs u(0,t) = u(1,t) = 0 and IC u(x,0) = sin(πx) was chosen because it has a known closed-form analytical solution:
\[u(x, t) = e^{-\alpha \pi^2 t} \sin(\pi x)\]
That choice is load-bearing. The BLR feature basis is [1, x, sin(πx), cos(πx)] — the analytical solution exp(-π²·0.1)·sin(πx) is exactly representable as a single non-zero coefficient on the sin(πx) basis function. So with enough samples, the BLR posterior mean must converge to the truth. If it doesn’t, the BLR implementation is broken — not the test. This is the same trick statisticians use to validate Bayesian implementations: pick a problem where the truth is in the basis, and the posterior is a closed-form limit.
The training schedule is deliberately asymmetric: 32 samples uniformly across [0.25, 0.75] (interior), 4 samples each in [0, 0.25) and [0.75, 1] (edges). Without this asymmetry, uniform training would give uniform leverage and the spatial-partition property would be invisible.
Data scale: 40 training samples total (32 interior + 8 edge), 5 probe points. Enough to demonstrate the spatial-partition property; not enough to claim ABNG matches state-of-the-art Bayesian PINN methods on realistic PDE problems. Validating against PINN benchmarks (NS equations, 2-D Burgers) at 10⁴+ collocation points is future work.


Code sketch
The PINN demo’s core loop, in CJC-Lang:
fn pinn_features_2d(x: f64) -> Tensor {
    Tensor.from_vec([1.0, x, sin(pi() * x), cos(pi() * x)], [1, 4])
}

fn analytical_u(x: f64) -> f64 {
    let decay = exp(-0.9869604401089358);  // exp(-π² · 0.1)
    decay * sin(pi() * x)
}

fn train_one(g: i64, x: f64) {
    let leaf = route_leaf(g, x);             // codebook descent
    let phi = pinn_features_2d(x);
    let y_val = analytical_u(x);             // ground truth
    let y = Tensor.from_vec([y_val], [1]);
    abng_blr_update(g, leaf, phi, y);        // NIG conjugate update
    abng_observe(g, leaf, y_val);
}
After training, a probe at x = 0.5 returns (mean ≈ 0.372, lev_low, ale_finite) — mean matches exp(-π²·0.1) · sin(π·0.5) = 0.372 to within 0.05. A probe at x = 0.05 returns (mean ≈ small, lev_high, ale_finite) — different leaf, less data, higher leverage.


Connection to existing techniques









Technique
Posterior representation
Cost
Decomposed epistemic/aleatoric?




Gaussian Processes
Full posterior over functions
O(n³) Cholesky
Yes (kernel + likelihood noise)


Deep ensembles
Variance across M trained nets
M× training cost
Approximately


MC dropout
Variational approx via dropout
Cheap, biased
No (entangled)


Conformal prediction
Distribution-free intervals
Cheap
No


ABNG per-leaf BLR
Closed-form NIG posterior
O(d²) per update
Yes (clean factor)



ABNG slots between GPs (full posterior, expensive) and conformal prediction (distribution-free, no decomposition). It’s most similar in spirit to Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (BART) — leaf-localized closed-form Bayesian fits — but with a quantile-codebook routing instead of stochastic tree growth, and a stronger determinism contract.


What this enables

Active learning in scientific computing — query the BLR for epistemic_leverage; sample where it’s highest.
Uncertainty-aware PINNs — refuse to predict in regions where leverage exceeds a threshold; fall back to a higher-fidelity solver. This is what the PINN community has been chasing via deep ensembles or HMC for years.
Heteroskedastic regression done right — the aleatoric/epistemic split lets you tell “this region is intrinsically noisy” from “I haven’t seen enough of this region yet.”
Uncertainty propagation downstream — feed the Student-t predictive into Monte Carlo / robust optimization pipelines.

The PINN literature has long treated Bayesian uncertainty as expensive infrastructure (HMC, variational PINNs). ABNG provides this as a closed-form per-leaf posterior — no inference machinery beyond a Cholesky update.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 2 — Per-leaf bounded scaling (GP-like, scalable)
Demo: Tabular regression on the unit square (tests/test_abng_tabular_gp_cjcl.rs + tabular_source.rs)

What this demo proves
Five distinct properties asserted as separate tests:

max(per_leaf_n_seen) < 0.9 × N — no single leaf holds the entire dataset. Evidence is partitioned, not duplicated.
sum(per_leaf_n_seen) == N — every training point routes to exactly one leaf. No double-counting; no points lost.
max_per_leaf_bounded — the partitioning works as designed. (This is the scalability claim’s empirical foundation.)
trained_mse < 0.5 × prior_mse — the BLR posterior actually learns. Without this, the structure-of-the-graph properties would be vacuous.
lev_shrinks_with_data — leverage at a fixed probe drops from a 16-pt-trained leaf to a 64-pt-trained leaf. GP-like uncertainty contraction reproduced.



The math (cost analysis)
ABNG’s per-leaf cost decomposes cleanly:







Operation
Cost




Codebook descent
O(n_dims · log(n_bins))


BLR conjugate update at one leaf
O(d² · n_leaf) (Cholesky-factor maintenance)


Predictive at query point
O(d²) (one Cholesky solve)


Aggregate cost across N points, L leaves
O(N · d²) — linear in N



Compare to a classical GP on the same N points:



Operation
Cost




Kernel matrix K_NN
O(N²) memory


Cholesky decomposition
O(N³) compute


Predictive variance per query
O(N²)



ABNG is asymptotically O(N · d²) vs GP’s O(N³). For d = 4 (the demo’s basis dim) and N = 10⁴: ABNG ≈ 1.6×10⁵ ops; GP ≈ 10¹² ops — seven orders of magnitude.
The trade-off: ABNG’s leaves are independent — no global covariance structure. For workloads where local Bayesian behavior is sufficient (most tabular ML), this is a feature, not a bug.


Why bilinear regression on the unit square
The target is y = 0.3 + 0.5x₁ + 0.4x₂ + 0.7·x₁·x₂. Three deliberate choices:

Exactly fittable in the BLR feature basis [1, x₁, x₂, x₁·x₂]. With enough samples, BLR posterior mean → exact coefficients [0.3, 0.5, 0.4, 0.7]. This makes the MSE-reduction test meaningful: any residual MSE comes from sample efficiency, not from the model class being wrong.
2-D input with a routing dimension. The codebook routes by x₁ (4 bins) and lets x₂ become a within-leaf feature. This mirrors the canonical ABNG pattern: use one dimension to partition the input space, regress on the rest within each leaf. Common in production: route by (time-of-day, geo-region) and regress on the rest within each segment.
Unit square domain — predictable, no scaling tricks. Codebook boundaries [0.25, 0.5, 0.75] evenly partition [0, 1].

Data scale: Two trained graphs (16-point and 64-point training sets) plus a 200-point graph for the per-leaf-bound check; 32 held-out test queries for MSE comparison. The asymptotic claim “ABNG O(N · d²) beats classical GP O(N³)” is correct by construction but not yet wall-clock-benchmarked at scales where it matters (n ≥ 10⁴). Without those numbers this is a “promising primitive,” not a measured speedup vs. e.g. SVGP / sklearn’s GaussianProcessRegressor / GPy.


Code sketch
fn build_tabular_graph(seed: i64) -> i64 {
    let g = abng_new(seed);
    let codebook = Tensor.from_vec([0.25, 0.5, 0.75], [1, 3]);
    abng_set_codebook(g, codebook);                          // 4 bins on x₁
    abng_set_leaf_head(g, 2, Tensor.from_vec([4.0], [1]), 1, "tanh");
    abng_set_blr_prior(g, 2.0, 1.0, 0.5);                    // NIG (a, b, σ_init)
    // ... add 4 children, one per codebook bin ...
    g
}

fn train_one(g: i64, x1: f64, x2: f64) {
    let leaf = route_for(g, x1);                             // x₁ → prefix → leaf
    let phi = Tensor.from_vec([1.0, x1, x2, x1 * x2], [1, 4]);
    let y_val = target(x1, x2);
    abng_blr_update(g, leaf, phi, Tensor.from_vec([y_val], [1]));
}
After 200 training points, every leaf has its own BLR posterior with mean → [0.3, 0.5, 0.4, 0.7] and increasingly sharp leverage. Sum of n_seen across all four leaves equals exactly 200; the largest leaf holds well under 90% of points.


Connection to existing techniques









Technique
Routing
Per-leaf model
Scaling




Classical GP
None (global)
Kernel
O(n³)


Sparse GP / SVGP
Inducing points
Kernel
O(n·m²) for m inducing pts


Mondrian Forest
Stochastic tree splits
Point estimate
O(n·log n)


BART
Stochastic tree splits
Bayesian posterior
O(n·log n) per tree


Locally-weighted regression
Distance-weighted
Linear fit
O(n) per query


ABNG
Quantile codebook (deterministic)
Closed-form NIG posterior
O(N · d²)



The closest analog is Mondrian Forests (Lakshminarayanan, Roy & Teh 2014) — both partition the input space and fit per-leaf models. The differences: Mondrian uses stochastic splits and point estimates; ABNG uses deterministic quantile boundaries and full closed-form Bayesian posteriors. ABNG also adds the audit chain that Mondrian doesn’t have.


What this enables

Drop-in tabular Bayesian regression at GP-prohibitive sizes (n > 10⁴ where dense Cholesky becomes painful)
Online / streaming learning — incremental BLR update is O(d²) per point, vs O(n²) incremental for classical GP rank-one updates
Snapshot-checkpointable Bayesian state — classical GPs don’t naturally serialize (you’d persist the full Cholesky factor + raw data); ABNG snapshots the entire posterior in one blob via the audit chain
Feature-engineered uncertainty quantification — the practitioner picks routing dimensions (time-of-day, region, customer segment) and the per-segment posteriors are independently calibrated







Warning



The asymptotic claim is correct by construction (the math says so) but not yet benchmarked against a classical GP at realistic sizes. Wall-clock benchmarks at n ∈ {10³, 10⁴, 10⁵} need to be run before treating ABNG as a “GP killer.”

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 3 — Tamper-evident training history (audit chain)
Demo: Universal property — every demo asserts it.

What every demo proves
Every state mutation in ABNG funnels through one chain step:
\[
\text{new\_hash} = \text{SHA-256}\bigl(\text{previous\_hash} \,\Vert\, \text{canonical\_payload}(\text{event})\bigr)
\]
Every demo asserts verify_chain == Ok after training. For an n-event audit log, verify_chain walks the chain forward, recomputing new_hash at every step from the stored previous hash and the stored canonical payload, and compares to the stored value. Any single byte changed in any payload would fail the recomputation at that event AND every subsequent event — a Merkle-style cascade.
Across the 11 demos, this code path runs hundreds of times. The fact that verify_chain == Ok after every demo’s training session is the empirical proof the audit chain is sound under realistic workloads.


The threat model
The audit chain is designed against three concrete attacks:

Post-hoc rewrite. Attacker modifies a historical training observation after the fact. Detection: the modified event’s new_hash no longer matches sha256(previous_hash ‖ payload), AND every subsequent event’s stored previous_hash no longer matches the recomputed-from-modified value. Cascading detection.
Event reordering. Attacker shuffles the order of training events. Detection: each event’s payload includes its seq number; reordering puts events at the wrong seq, breaking the per-event monotonic-seq invariant.
Event substitution. Attacker replaces one event with another that produces the same new_hash. Detection: SHA-256 collision resistance — finding two payloads with the same hash is computationally infeasible.

Data scale: Universal property — every demo’s audit log (3 to ~110 events, depending on workload) gets verified. The largest audit log in the test suite is the PINN demo’s 110-event chain post-asymmetric training. The cryptographic argument scales independently of dataset size: SHA-256 chain verification is O(log(events)) per check and the security guarantees come from the hash function itself, not from the workload. So this is the one capability where “small data demos suffice” is a load-bearing claim, not a hedge.


Code sketch — what an event payload looks like
[seq u64 BE | epoch u64 BE | node_id u32 BE | kind tag u8 |
 kind body (variable) |
 stats_version u64 BE | stats_hash [u8; 32]]
For a BeliefUpdate event (the most common kind, fired on every abng_observe), the kind body is a single value f64-bits BE — 8 bytes. Total payload for a BeliefUpdate is 21 + 8 + 8 + 32 = 69 bytes, hashed into the chain.
The canonical encoding is byte-deterministic: big-endian on every numeric field, fixed sizes, no padding ambiguity. Two ABNG instances run with the same seed and observation order produce identical event payload bytes — and therefore identical chain hashes.


Connection to existing techniques









Technique
Hash-chain
ML-aware
Per-prediction lineage




Git
Yes (DAG of commits)
No
No


MLflow
No (timestamped log)
Yes
Manual


sigstore / in-toto
Yes (artifact attestation)
No
Per-artifact


Blockchain ML provenance
Yes (full chain on-chain)
Yes
Yes, but expensive


ABNG audit chain
Yes (linear)
Yes (every state mutation)
Yes, baked in



The closest analog is in-toto attestations in software supply-chain land — except in-toto is per-artifact (each release gets one attestation), while ABNG is per-event (every observation, every weight update).


What this enables

Forensic post-incident analysis — “Was this model trained on the bad data?” Replay the chain, check.
Regulatory replay — the model’s full training history is one cryptographically-linked blob.
Reproducibility certification — papers ship (model.snap, dataset) pairs; reviewers reproduce + verify chain in one byte comparison.
ML supply chain attestation — fine-tuning extends the chain; provenance of every weight is traceable.
Insider-threat detection — anyone modifying historical events breaks every subsequent hash, visible to anyone running verify_chain.

This is closer to Git’s content-addressed model than to traditional ML metadata stores. MLflow / Weights & Biases give you external logs you have to trust; ABNG gives you a model artifact whose history is cryptographically self-attesting.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 4 — Cryptographic dataset attestation
Demo: Lineage attestation with synthetic clinical-trial data (tests/test_abng_lineage_attestation_cjcl.rs + lineage_source.rs)

What this demo proves
The headline test (lineage_attestation_three_signals_independent) proves that tampering one row of a 64-row dataset (changing patient #17’s response from 0.525 to 0.999) produces three independent SHA-256 divergences:

chain_head divergence — every audit event’s hash cascades from row 17 onward.
BLR state_hash divergence — the leaf where the tampered row routes has a different posterior.
provenance_stamp divergence — the dataset fingerprint differs by construction.

The “independent” qualifier is doing real work. Even if an attacker forged one signal (e.g., recomputed the chain to hide the change), the other two would still surface the tamper.


The threat model and why three signals matter
The classic attack against ML provenance is collusion: the attacker controls the training process, so they can produce a consistent-but-fake audit trail. Three independent signals raise the cost:








Attack
Defeats which signal?
Defeats all three?




Modify training data, recompute chain
chain_head ✓
No — BLR state diverges


Modify training data, retrain to match BLR state
chain_head ✓, BLR state ✓
No — provenance stamp diverges


Modify training data, retrain, restamp with new fingerprint
All three forgeable internally
But: fingerprint is publicly verifiable against the dataset bytes



The provenance stamp is sha256(canonical_dataset_bytes). An auditor with the original dataset can recompute this themselves — the attacker can’t forge it without also forging the dataset (which is what we’re trying to detect in the first place). The chain and BLR state are internal consistency checks; the provenance stamp is the external binding to the dataset.


Why a synthetic clinical-trial workload

Maps directly to a real industry threat model. “Lab A trains a model claiming approved dataset; attacker substitutes contaminated dataset” is the FDA model-attestation problem in one sentence.
64-row size is realistic for Phase II clinical-trial cohorts (often <200 patients per arm).
Cubic dose-response curve y = 0.2 + 0.6·dose + 0.1·dose² is biologically plausible AND exactly fittable in the BLR basis [1, dose, dose², dose³], giving us ground-truth assertions in addition to the security claims.
Tamper at row 17 specifically because dose=0.5 falls right at a codebook bin boundary — routing the tampered observation to a specific leaf, making the BLR state_hash divergence clearly attributable.

Data scale: Two graphs trained on 16-row datasets each (the CJC-Lang version of the demo uses 16 rows for interpreter speed; the Rust-side version uses 64). Tampering one row out of 16 — or one out of 64 — is the minimum-effort attack. A 1.5%-of-dataset perturbation is the strongest test possible: if even this is detectable, larger tampers are detected a fortiori. Scale-up to 10⁴ - 10⁶ row clinical-trial datasets is straightforward (the cryptographic argument is dataset-size-invariant) but not yet validated end-to-end with a real anonymized clinical-trial corpus.


Code sketch
fn train_and_stamp(seed: i64, rows: Any, fingerprint: String) -> i64 {
    let g = build_lineage_graph(seed);
    abng_stamp_provenance(g, 0, fingerprint);                // sha256(dataset_bytes)
    let n = len(rows);
    let i = 0;
    while i < n {
        let row = rows[i];
        let dose = row[0];
        let resp = row[1];
        let leaf = route_leaf(g, dose);
        let phi = Tensor.from_vec(
            [1.0, dose, dose * dose, dose * dose * dose], [1, 4]);
        abng_blr_update(g, leaf, phi, Tensor.from_vec([resp], [1]));
        abng_observe(g, leaf, resp);
        i = i + 1;
    }
    g
}
The two graphs g_a and g_b are built identically — only the row data and fingerprint differ. Comparing (chain_head, blr_state_hash[node 3], provenance_stamp) between them yields three different hashes.


Connection to existing techniques









Technique
Cryptographic
ML-integrated
Per-prediction




MLflow / W&B logs
No
Yes
No


Sigstore / in-toto
Yes
No (artifact-only)
No


Datasheet for Datasets (Gebru et al. 2018)
No (process-only)
Yes
No


Model Cards (Mitchell et al. 2019)
No
Yes
No


MLOps governance frameworks
Variable
Yes
Manual


ABNG provenance chain
Yes
Yes
Yes (baked into predict_snap)



ABNG’s contribution is the combination: cryptographic, ML-integrated, AND per-prediction. None of the existing tools do all three in one artifact.


What this enables







Use case
Concrete flow




FDA model attestation (pharma)
Lab runs trial → trains ABNG → stamps with sha256(approved_dataset). Regulator: cjcl abng explain --model X.snap pred.snap checks all three signals in one CLI invocation


Financial model audit (SEC, Basel III)
“Prove this risk model used only data through model-validation cutoff” — chain head + dataset fingerprint answer in one byte comparison


HIPAA-compliant healthcare ML
Patient-consent boundaries enforced cryptographically: stamp = sha256(consent_set_v2_canonicalized)


GDPR Article 22
Right to explanation = “show me the audit trail of training data that produced this decision about me”


Academic reproducibility
paper.pdf + model.snap + dataset.csv — anyone with all three can verify byte-for-byte


Insider-threat in ML supply chain
Attacker who fine-tunes a base model on contaminated data CAN’T hide the audit-chain extension



The closest non-ABNG analog is in-toto-attested ML pipelines (an active research area, see Torres-Arias et al. 2019). The difference: ABNG bakes attestation inside the model artifact, not as a sidecar.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 5 — OOD detection composite
Demo: Asymmetric codebook with deliberately-missing children (tests/test_abng_ood_detection_cjcl.rs + ood_source.rs)

What this demo proves
The headline assertion is three-tier separation:
max(ood_score in dense bins)  <  ood_score in sparse bin  <  ood_score in routing-fall-off bin
Three evidence regimes are cleanly partitioned. The composite is a function of three independent inputs.


The math
ABNG’s ood_score composites three signals:
\[\text{ood}(x) = f\bigl(\text{density}(x),\ \text{epistemic\_z}(x),\ \text{prefix\_distance}(x)\bigr)\]
where:








Signal
Formula
What it detects




density(x)
\(1 - \exp(-d^2)\) where \(d^2 = (x - \bar{\mu})^\top \text{diag}(\Sigma)^{-1}(x - \bar{\mu})\)
Distance from training cluster mean (per-leaf)


epistemic_z(x)
\(\min(\varphi^\top \Lambda_n^{-1}\varphi / \mathbb{E}[\text{lev}], 1)\)
Calibrated BLR uncertainty (per-leaf)


prefix_distance(x)
\((\text{prefix\_max} - \text{matched\_prefix}) / \text{prefix\_max}\)
Routing-failure rate (per-graph)



The composite combines these into a single bounded [0, 1] score. Each signal catches a different OOD mode:

Density catches inputs that fall in a known leaf but far from its training cluster.
Epistemic catches inputs where the BLR posterior says “I’m uncertain here.”
Prefix distance catches inputs that don’t fit the codebook structure at all — i.e. the descent fell off the tree because no child exists at that prefix byte.



Why deliberately omit one child
A symmetric setup (children in all 4 bins, varying training density) would only exercise the density and epistemic signals — not prefix_distance. By deliberately not adding a child for bin 3, queries in bin 3 descend with matched_prefix=0, and the prefix-distance contribution to the composite fires.
The demo asserts the composite works, not just individual signals. If it only varied density, you’d be left wondering whether the routing-distance contribution is real or just a docstring claim. The asymmetric child set proves it’s real.
Data scale: 32 dense observations per bin on bins 0 and 1, 4 sparse on bin 2, 0 on bin 3 (no leaf) — 68 total density observations. 4 probe queries (one per bin). Sufficient to demonstrate the three-tier separation property; insufficient to characterize behavior under realistic ML inputs (e.g., 1024-D image embeddings, NLP token vectors). OOD detection on high-D unstructured inputs is fundamentally outside the codebook-routing paradigm — ABNG’s OOD score requires structured input where the codebook can produce a meaningful prefix encoding.


Code sketch
fn probe_ood(g: i64, x: f64) -> f64 {
    let x_t = Tensor.from_vec([x], [1]);
    let prefix = abng_encode_prefix(g, x_t);
    let evidence = abng_descend(g, prefix);
    let matched = int(evidence.get([0]));      // matched_prefix
    let leaf = int(evidence.get([1]));         // resolved leaf id
    let phi = features_1d(x);
    abng_ood_score(g, leaf, phi, matched, 1)   // composite [0, 1]
}

// Build phase: add children for bins 0, 1, 2 — NOT bin 3.
abng_add_node(g, 0, 0);   // bin 0 child
abng_add_node(g, 0, 1);   // bin 1 child
abng_add_node(g, 0, 2);   // bin 2 child
// (no add_node for byte 3 — bin 3 queries descend with matched_prefix = 0)

// Train density on bins 0 and 1 heavily, bin 2 lightly:
train_leaf_density(g, 1, 0.125, 32);   // bin 0: dense
train_leaf_density(g, 2, 0.375, 32);   // bin 1: dense
train_leaf_density(g, 3, 0.625, 4);    // bin 2: sparse
// bin 3: no training (no leaf)
After training, probing at x = 0.10 (bin 0, dense) gives a low ood; x = 0.40 (bin 1, dense) low; x = 0.62 (bin 2, sparse) medium; x = 0.90 (bin 3, no leaf) highest.


Connection to existing techniques








Technique
OOD signal
Bayesian-aware?




Mahalanobis distance OOD (Lee et al. 2018)
Distance from class means
No


Energy-based OOD (Liu et al. 2020)
-logsumexp(logits)
No


Deep ensemble disagreement
Variance across M models
Approximately


ODIN (Liang et al. 2018)
Temperature + input perturbation
No


Conformal prediction p-values
Distribution-free coverage
No


ABNG composite
density + epistemic + routing
Yes (BLR posterior is principled)



The closest is Mahalanobis OOD — both compute a distance-from-training-cluster score. ABNG adds the routing-failure signal on top, which catches a class of inputs Mahalanobis can’t distinguish (in-cluster but topologically out-of-routing).


What this enables

Self-driving / robotics — abstain on inputs unfamiliar to the training distribution; hand off to safe-mode.
Medical diagnosis — don’t predict on patient profiles outside the training population.
Fraud detection — novel attack patterns trigger high OOD scores.
Active learning — high-OOD inputs are highest-value labeling candidates.
Safety-critical ML — defense-in-depth against distribution shift at inference time, not just monitoring.

A query can be OOD because (a) it’s far from training points (density), (b) the model is uncertain there (epistemic), or (c) it doesn’t fit the codebook structure at all (prefix). Each tells a different operational story — and the composite collapses them into one actionable score.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 6 — Adaptive structural triggers
Demo: Two-sibling shared-signature topology (tests/test_abng_adaptive_triggers_cjcl.rs + adaptive_source.rs)

What this demo proves
After running abng_decide_step three times on a graph with two sibling children:

merge_count == 1 — exactly one Merge action fires (deterministic given the canary thresholds).
audit_log_grew — the structural mutation is recorded as an event in the chain.
verify_chain == Ok — the chain integrity holds after structural mutation.
chain_head matches the Rust-side decide_step_canary BYTE-FOR-BYTE — this is the most striking property in the entire demo set.

That last point deserves emphasis. The Rust-side decide_step_canary_tests.rs locks chain_head = d064fb08…7807. The CJC-Lang demo, running through both AST eval AND MIR exec, produces the same hex. The same workload, expressed three different ways (direct Rust API, CJC-Lang AST, CJC-Lang MIR), produces bit-identical SHA-256 output through 23+ events.


The math behind the Merge trigger
decide_step evaluates each node against six trigger conditions in order: Compress → Merge → Split → Prune → Grow → Freeze. For the Merge trigger specifically, ABNG checks two conditions on a pair of siblings (i, j):

Signature similarity: Hamming distance of the 8-byte truncated SHA-256 signatures < tau_merge. The signature is computed from a 4-channel Welford profile (prediction, uncertainty, calibration, routing). Similar profiles → similar signatures → low Hamming distance.
Posterior similarity (KL divergence): \[
\mathrm{KL}\bigl(\mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \Sigma_i)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(\mu_j, \Sigma_j)\bigr) < \text{kl\_merge}
\] where (μᵢ, Σᵢ) are the BLR posterior parameters at node i. This is the standard multivariate Gaussian KL.

Both conditions must hold for Merge to fire. The Merge action then folds j’s observations into i via Chan/Golub/LeVeque parallel Welford merge — preserving the post-merge stats as if they’d been observed in series.


Why the two-sibling shared-signature topology

Mirrors the Rust-side decide_step_canary exactly — root + 2 children at keys 1 and 2, three observations at root (0.10, 0.25, 0.40), three decide_step calls. This lets the CJC-Lang demo serve as a direct cross-pipeline determinism gate.
Merge is the most reliably-triggered action. With identical training distributions on the two siblings, their signatures align trivially (Hamming distance = 0). Decision-policy thresholds tau_merge=0 and kl_merge=0.1 are satisfied immediately — KL between fresh-prior posteriors is exactly zero.
Small graph keeps the test fast — 4 nodes, 3 observations, 3 decide_step calls. Runs in milliseconds even through the AST interpreter.

Data scale: 3 observations + 3 decide_step calls, on a 4-node graph. This is deliberately the smallest demo in the entire suite — its job is to prove the trigger semantics fire deterministically and that the chain_head matches the Rust-side canary byte-for-byte. It’s not a demonstration that ABNG-driven structural adaptation is competitive against e.g. Mondrian Forests on 10⁵-row tabular benchmarks. That comparison is genuine future work — engineering a Grow-firing or Split-firing workload at scale, then measuring vs. baseline tree learners.


Code sketch
fn main() {
    let g = build_adaptive_graph(42);

    abng_observe(g, 0, 0.10);
    abng_observe(g, 0, 0.25);
    abng_observe(g, 0, 0.40);

    let i = 0;
    while i < 3 {
        let counts = abng_decide_step(g);    // [grow, split, merge, prune, compress, freeze]
        i = i + 1;
    }

    let merge_count = abng_action_count(g, 2);  // → 1
    print("merge_count: " + to_string(merge_count));
    print("chain_head: " + abng_chain_head(g));  // → d064fb08…7807
}


Connection to existing techniques









Technique
Adaptive structure?
Triggers
Domain




Mondrian Forests (Lakshminarayanan 2014)
Yes (stochastic splits)
Online
Tabular


Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (Chipman 2010)
Yes (stochastic)
Batch
Tabular


Hoeffding Trees / VFDT (Domingos 2000)
Yes (statistical)
Streaming
Tabular


Concept-drift adaptive learners (ADWIN, Page-Hinkley)
Yes (drift-triggered)
Streaming
Various


Neural Architecture Search (Zoph 2017)
Yes (optimization-based)
Offline
Deep learning


ABNG decide_step
Yes (deterministic, audit-tracked)
Six action types
Bayesian belief graphs



The closest analog is Hoeffding Trees — both fire structural changes based on running statistics. The differences: Hoeffding Trees use Hoeffding-bound-based split tests; ABNG uses fixed thresholds on a 14-element decision-policy tensor. ABNG also adds the audit-chain integration that lets you prove what structural changes happened and when.


What this enables

Self-organizing ML models — graph capacity scales with workload complexity; no manual architecture design.
Streaming / online learning — non-stationary distributions cause Grow on new modes, Prune on dead branches.
Memory-efficient ML — Compress unused subtrees; Merge redundant leaves; Prune inactive nodes.
Hierarchical model distillation — stable subtrees can be Frozen and exported as compiled MLPs.
Continual learning without catastrophic forgetting — Freeze stable-signature subtrees while training others.
AutoML topology search — decision-policy thresholds are hyperparameters; the trigger ladder is the search algorithm.







Warning



Honest caveat: the demo proves Merge fires deterministically. The other five trigger types (Grow, Split, Prune, Compress, Freeze) are tested at the primitive level but not demonstrated firing in a realistic workload. The “graph adapts to workload complexity” claim is half-substantiated by current demos. Closing this gap is straightforward (more demos with engineered workloads that fire each trigger type) but not yet shipped.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 7 — Calibration / ECE
Demo: Hand-constructed well-calibrated vs miscalibrated outcomes (tests/test_abng_calibration_cjcl.rs + calibration_source.rs)

What this demo proves
Two graphs receive 30 calibration observations each. Same predicted probabilities (p ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 0.9}, 10 each), different outcome distributions:








Graph
Outcome convention
Expected ECE




A: well-calibrated
predicted=0.1 → 1 of 10 positive; predicted=0.9 → 9 of 10 positive
< 0.05


B: miscalibrated
predicted=0.1 → 9 of 10 positive (FLIPPED); predicted=0.9 → 1 of 10 positive
> 0.5



The headline assertion ece_a < ece_b separates the two cleanly. Plus the specific bounds: ece_a < 0.05 (well-calibrated achieves near-zero ECE) and ece_b > 0.5 (flipped outcomes produce huge ECE).


The math
ABNG uses standard 15-bin reliability-binning ECE (Naeini, Cooper, Hauskrecht 2015):
\[
\text{ECE} = \sum_{b=1}^{15} \frac{|B_b|}{N} \,\bigl|\,\text{accuracy}(B_b) - \text{confidence}(B_b)\,\bigr|
\]
where:

B_b is the set of predictions falling in bin b (15 equal-width bins on [0, 1])
accuracy(B_b) = fraction of correct predictions in bin b
confidence(B_b) = mean predicted probability in bin b
|B_b| / N = bin’s empirical weight

A perfectly calibrated model has accuracy(B_b) = confidence(B_b) for every populated bin → ECE = 0. The miscalibrated graph with flipped outcomes pushes accuracy ≈ 1 - confidence in each bin → ECE saturates near 0.8.


Why “flip outcomes” as the miscalibration model

Maximum-ECE case. Predicting 90% confidence and being right 10% of the time is the worst possible outcome — confidence is anti-correlated with truth. This makes the test gate ece_b > 0.5 reliable; small numerical jitter doesn’t push the result close to the threshold.
Same prediction distribution → isolates the calibration signal. Both graphs see the same (p, y) pairs modulo y. Any difference in ECE is attributable to outcomes, not to predictions or to bin populations.
Three reliability bins (0.1, 0.5, 0.9) span low/mid/high probability range without requiring 100+ observations to populate every bin.

Data scale: 30 calibration observations per graph (2 graphs total = 60 observations). This is the minimum sufficient sample size to make the three-bin reliability statistics non-trivially populated and the ECE separation ece_a < 0.05 < 0.5 < ece_b reliable. It does not validate calibration on actual classification tasks (image classifiers, transformer logits, etc.) — those workloads have thousands of (p, y) pairs and 10-100 calibration buckets. Per-leaf ECE on a real classifier is straightforward future work.


Code sketch
fn well_calibrated_run(g: i64, node: i64) {
    // For predicted_prob = 0.1: 1 positive, 9 negative (10% base rate).
    let i = 0;
    while i < 10 {
        if i < 1 { abng_calibration_observe(g, node, 0.1, true); }
        if i >= 1 { abng_calibration_observe(g, node, 0.1, false); }
        i = i + 1;
    }
    // ... same pattern for p=0.5 (5/10) and p=0.9 (9/10) ...
}

fn miscalibrated_run(g: i64, node: i64) {
    // For predicted_prob = 0.1: 9 positive, 1 negative (FLIPPED).
    // For predicted_prob = 0.9: 1 positive, 9 negative (FLIPPED).
    // ... see source for full pattern ...
}

let ece_a = abng_calibration_ece(g_a, 0);     // < 0.05
let ece_b = abng_calibration_ece(g_b, 0);     // > 0.5


Connection to existing techniques








Technique
Calibration
Per-leaf?




Platt scaling (Platt 1999)
Post-hoc logistic regression on logits
No (global)


Isotonic regression (Zadrozny 2002)
Monotonic piecewise-constant fit
No (global)


Temperature scaling (Guo et al. 2017)
Single-parameter softmax rescale
No (global)


Beta calibration (Kull et al. 2017)
Beta distribution fit
No (global)


Histogram binning (Zadrozny 2001)
Per-bin frequency estimate
Possible per-leaf


ABNG calibration
15-bin reliability + ECE
Yes (per-node)



ABNG’s calibration is per-node — different leaves can be independently calibrated. This matters in practice: a model can be well-calibrated for European customers but miscalibrated for Asian customers (different leaves of the codebook). Per-leaf ECE makes this visible; global ECE would average it out.


What this enables

Risk-sensitive deployment — a poorly-calibrated 95%-confident prediction is operationally indistinguishable from an honest 60%-confident one — but you’ll act on it differently.
Threshold tuning — “should I act on predictions above 0.7?” depends on whether 0.7-bin predictions are actually correct 70% of the time.
Model selection in production — beyond accuracy/F1: ECE is the second-order quality metric.
Active learning targeting — high-ECE bins are evidence the model needs more training data in that probability range.
Per-segment calibration analysis — “is my model better calibrated for some user segments than others?” is answerable in one query.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 8 — Distribution-drift detection
Demo: Mean shift from 0 to 0.8 on a 4-D density tracker (tests/test_abng_drift_detection_cjcl.rs + drift_source.rs)

What this demo proves
A three-phase workload: train baseline, freeze, observe under same distribution, observe under shifted distribution. Three assertions:

drift_grew — drift_score(post-shift) > drift_score(same-dist).
meaningful_shift — drift_score(post-shift) > 2 × drift_score(same-dist). Catches a regression where drift detection becomes noisy.
drift_nonneg — both scores are non-negative (no NaN, no negative noise).



The math
ABNG’s drift detection is built on a streaming diagonal-Gaussian density tracker. For features φ ∈ ℝᵈ, the tracker maintains:

Running mean: μ̂ updated via Welford
Running diagonal covariance: Σ̂_diag updated via Welford on (φᵢ - μ̂)²

freeze_drift_baseline(node) snapshots (μ̂_baseline, Σ̂_diag_baseline) at a reference point (typically end of pre-deployment training). After freezing, drift_score measures the divergence between the current tracker and the frozen baseline:
\[
\text{drift\_score} = \bigl\|(\hat{\mu}_{\text{cur}} - \hat{\mu}_{\text{baseline}})\bigr\|^2_{\text{Mahal}} + \alpha \cdot \bigl\|\Sigma_{\text{cur,diag}} / \Sigma_{\text{baseline,diag}} - \mathbf{1}\bigr\|^2
\]
— a weighted combination of mean shift (under baseline covariance) and covariance ratio deviation. Pure mean shift (this demo’s case) gives a score dominated by the first term.


Why a pure mean shift

Translation, not rescaling isolates the mean-shift contribution to drift_score. A combined “scale and rotate” shift would test more, but the basic mean-shift case is the simplest sufficient demonstration.
0 to 0.8 is large enough to be unambiguous. Both distributions have ~0.01 jitter; shifting the center by 0.8 is 80x the jitter — the drift signal saturates well above the floor.
Same density-tracker geometry — same dimension count (4-D features), same covariance scale, same batch sizes (16 each post-baseline). Only the location changes.

Data scale: 32 baseline samples + 16 same-distribution + 16 shifted = 64 density observations total, on a single node. This proves the drift_score machinery responds correctly to a controlled mean shift; it does not characterize behavior under realistic concept drift (gradual drift, abrupt drift, recurring drift, periodic shift). Production ML drift detection is typically validated on streaming corpora with millions of points across months of real customer behavior — that’s a workload-grade benchmark, not a research demo, and it’s explicitly future work.


Code sketch
let g = build_drift_graph(7);

// Phase 0: train baseline density on N(0, 0.01).
let baseline_batch = density_batch(0.0, 32);
abng_density_observe(g, 0, baseline_batch);

abng_freeze_drift_baseline(g, 0);     // snapshot reference state

// Phase A: same distribution.
let same_batch = density_batch(0.0, 16);
abng_density_observe(g, 0, same_batch);
let drift_low = abng_drift_score(g, 0);

// Phase B: shifted distribution N(0.8, 0.01).
let shifted_batch = density_batch(0.8, 16);
abng_density_observe(g, 0, shifted_batch);
let drift_high = abng_drift_score(g, 0);

// drift_high > 2 * drift_low (the demo's headline assertion)


Connection to existing techniques









Technique
Statistic
Streaming?
Reference required




Kolmogorov-Smirnov test
Max CDF difference
No
Yes


Population Stability Index (PSI)
Discrete distribution distance
No (batch)
Yes


Maximum Mean Discrepancy (Gretton 2012)
Kernel-based difference
Possible
Yes


Page-Hinkley test
Change-point detection
Yes
Implicit


ADWIN (Bifet 2007)
Adaptive windowing
Yes
None (auto-detect)


DDM (Gama 2004)
Drift Detection Method (error rate)
Yes
None


ABNG drift_score
Mahalanobis-style on density tracker
Streaming
Frozen baseline



The closest analog is PSI (widely used in finance) — both compute a deviation-from-baseline distribution score. The differences: PSI is for discrete histograms; ABNG operates on continuous diagonal-Gaussian density. PSI is computed batch; ABNG accumulates streamingly via Welford.


What this enables

Production ML monitoring — alert ops when input distribution drifts from training; trigger retraining.
Continual learning auto-trigger — drift signal is the cue to incorporate new data into the model.
A/B test data validation — confirm the new dataset’s distribution matches the original before deploying a new model.
Sensor / pipeline integrity — detect upstream data-source switches, sensor drift, schema changes.
Customer-behavior modeling — recommender systems trained on Q1 data drifting against Q3 user behavior.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 9 — Log compaction
Demo: Multi-node observation stream + compact_log (tests/test_abng_compact_log_cjcl.rs + compact_source.rs)

What this demo proves
Five training observations across three nodes, then compact_log fires:

emitted == 3 — exactly one StatsSnapshot per touched node.
audit_grew_by_emitted — audit_post == audit_pre + emitted.
chain_advanced — chain_head changed (events were properly chain-linked).
verify_chain == Ok post-compact — the new events don’t break integrity.



The math (and what’s deferred)
compact_log(g, until_seq) walks events in [0, until_seq), tracks the set of distinct node IDs touched, and for each emits one StatsSnapshot event whose payload is (node_id, sha256(canonical_bytes(node.stats))).
This sets up the read-side optimization that ships in a future phase: when smart_replay encounters a StatsSnapshot, it can:

Trust the snapshot’s stats_hash matches the node’s canonical_bytes (consistency check, shipped today).
Skip the node.observe(value) mutation for subsequent BeliefUpdate events in the same node’s pre-snapshot range, since the snapshot subsumes their cumulative effect.
Install the stats directly from the per-node section’s canonical_bytes instead of replaying from observations.

The shipped layer is the marker emission + chain-integrity preservation + smart_replay consistency check. The cycle-saving fast-forward is Phase 0.6 work.


Code sketch
abng_observe(g, 0, 0.1);
abng_observe(g, 0, 0.2);
abng_observe(g, 1, 0.5);
abng_observe(g, 1, 0.6);
abng_observe(g, 2, 0.9);

let audit_pre = abng_audit_len(g);             // e.g. 8
let emitted = abng_compact_log(g, audit_pre);  // → 3 (3 touched nodes)
let audit_post = abng_audit_len(g);            // 8 + 3 = 11
print("verify_chain: " + to_string(abng_verify_chain(g)));  // → true
Data scale: 5 observations across 3 nodes (smallest in the suite). This proves the marker emission is deterministic and chain-integrity-preserving. The interesting scale claim — “compact_log + smart-replay can fast-forward replay of a 10⁶-event audit log” — is deferred to Phase 0.6 along with the actual cycle-saving optimization.


Connection to existing techniques








Technique
Mechanism
Domain




Database WAL compaction
Replace old WAL records with snapshot
RDBMS


LSM tree compaction
Merge sorted runs
KV stores (RocksDB, Cassandra)


Chandy-Lamport snapshotting
Distributed-system state capture
Distributed systems


Checkpoint-restart
Periodic full-state save
HPC, ML training


ABNG compact_log + StatsSnapshot
Event-log markers, hash-chain-integrated
ML training audit logs



The closest analog is WAL log compaction in databases — periodic snapshot points that let you truncate older log entries without losing recoverability. ABNG adds the chain-integrity property: the snapshot itself is a hash-linked event, so post-compaction you can still cryptographically verify nothing was tampered with.


What this enables

Long-running training without log explosion — periodic compaction preserves checkpoints while bounding audit-log growth.
Snapshot-based debugging — each StatsSnapshot is a “save point” for forensic replay.
Foundation for the Phase 0.6 fast-forward optimization — smart-replay can fast-forward past observation runs by trusting the StatsSnapshot.
Distributed training checkpointing — workers compact-log periodically; central server replays the compact form.







Warning



The cycle-saving optimization is deferred to Phase 0.6. Today the marker layer ships with chain-integrity preservation; the actual fast-forward that would make this user-visibly faster lands later.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 10 — Maturity observability
Demo: Stable observation stream + decide_step (tests/test_abng_maturity_inspection_cjcl.rs + maturity_source.rs)

What this demo proves
Read abng_node_maturity at two timepoints: fresh graph (T=0) and after 10 stable observations + 5 decide_step calls (T=1). Five assertions:

sum_at_t0 == 0.0 — fresh graph, all maturity flags are zero.
maturity_evolved — sum at T=1 strictly exceeds sum at T=0.
calib_binary AND unc_binary — positions [1] (calibration_stable) and [2] (uncertainty_stable) are strict 0/1 flags.
samples_seen_nonneg — position [0] (samples_seen) is non-negative.
trust_in_range — position [3] (trust_level) is in [0, 1].



The semantics
abng_node_maturity(g, node) returns a 4-element f64 tensor:









Index
Field
Type
Definition




0
samples_seen
non-negative count
Total observations applied to this node


1
calibration_stable
binary 0/1
True if 3-window ECE history’s \|Δ\| < ece_stability_max_delta


2
uncertainty_stable
binary 0/1
True if 3-window σ history shows ratio < sigma_stability_ratio


3
trust_level
continuous [0, 1]
Composite trust score from signature stability + maturity flags



The flags advance through decide_step calls plus the underlying signature_stable_calls counter that increments on consecutive matching signatures.


Why a stable observation stream
The maturity flags advance under two conditions:

signature_stable_calls increments when consecutive decide_step calls observe an unchanged signature
samples_seen simply counts observations

By choosing identical observations (abng_observe(g, n, 0.1) repeatedly), the signature stays exactly identical across decide_step calls — the stability counter climbs deterministically. With 10 observations + 5 decide_step calls, at least the stability flags should flip.
Realistic data (varying observations) would also work but with lower predictability — the signature might wobble enough that signature_stable_calls resets occasionally. The stable-stream choice makes the demo deterministic.
Data scale: 10 observations + 5 decide_step calls. Deliberately tiny — the workload exists to prove “maturity flags evolve from 0 → 1 under stable training,” not to characterize maturity dynamics under realistic noisy ML training. A real production-monitoring deployment would observe maturity over thousands of decide_step calls across hours/days of training.


Code sketch
let g = build_maturity_graph(42);

let mat0 = abng_node_maturity(g, 0);  // [0, 0, 0, 0] — fresh

let i = 0;
while i < 10 {
    abng_observe(g, 0, 0.1);
    abng_observe(g, 1, 0.1);
    i = i + 1;
}
let j = 0;
while j < 5 {
    abng_decide_step(g);
    j = j + 1;
}

let mat1 = abng_node_maturity(g, 0);
let r0 = mat1.get([0]);  // samples_seen (count > 0)
let r1 = mat1.get([1]);  // calibration_stable (0 or 1)
let r2 = mat1.get([2]);  // uncertainty_stable (0 or 1)
let r3 = mat1.get([3]);  // trust_level (continuous [0, 1])


Connection to existing techniques








Technique
Convergence diagnostic
Domain




Gelman-Rubin R̂
Cross-chain variance ratio
MCMC convergence


Effective Sample Size (ESS)
Autocorrelation-based
MCMC convergence


Validation-loss plateau detection
Loss derivative threshold
Deep learning training


EarlyStopping callback
Patience + monitor metric
sklearn/keras training


ML monitoring stacks (Arize, WhyLabs)
External time-series dashboards
Production ML


ABNG node_maturity
Per-node 4-tuple
Bayesian belief graphs



The closest analog in ML practice is EarlyStopping + production monitoring — both ask “has my model converged enough to deploy?” ABNG’s contribution: per-node granularity (you can see which leaves have stabilized) and cryptographic integrity (the maturity values are derived from the same audit-tracked state, not an external log).


What this enables

Production ML monitoring dashboard — “has my model converged?” → check maturity per-node.
Auto-stop training — stop when maturity flags have all stabilized (avoid wasted compute).
Hyperparameter tuning — which learning rate makes flags settle fastest?
Convergence-failure debugging — which flag never reaches 1? — points at the failing component.
Production rollout gating — deploy-time check: only ship models where calibration_stable == 1.
Federated learning aggregation — aggregate worker updates only from nodes where maturity has stabilized.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Capability 11 — End-to-end determinism
Demo: Universal property — every CJC-Lang demo asserts it.

What every demo proves
Every CJC-Lang demo runs through both cjc-eval (AST tree-walk) AND cjc-mir-exec (MIR register-machine), then asserts byte-equal printed output. Across the 13 ABNG demo files, this constitutes ~95 byte-equal parity assertions over a vocabulary of ~30 abng_* builtins.
The strongest single instance: the adaptive triggers demo’s chain_head matches the Rust-side decide_step_canary byte-for-byte (d064fb08…7807). Same workload, three pipelines (direct Rust API + AST eval + MIR exec), bit-identical SHA-256 over 23+ audit events.


The architectural choices that make this work
Three load-bearing decisions enforced across the workspace:

Kahan compensated summation for all FP reductions (KahanAccumulatorF64). Naive Σxᵢ accumulates rounding error in O(n·ε); Kahan reduces this to O(ε). More importantly, Kahan is order-deterministic for fixed input order — no SIMD-introduced reordering, no FMA fusion. This is the single biggest source of “approximately deterministic” failure in standard ML stacks.
BTreeMap/BTreeSet only, never HashMap. Hash maps with random hashing have iteration order that depends on the per-process seed — different runs can iterate in different orders, and any code that depends on iteration order (e.g., reduction loops) becomes non-deterministic. BTreeMap iterates in sorted key order, every time.
SplitMix64 with explicit seed threading. No thread-local randomness, no time-based seeds, no platform-specific entropy. Every random draw is reproducible from (initial_seed, operation_history).

These three choices aren’t aspirational — they’re enforced by the parity gates. If anyone introduces FMA in cjc-runtime/linalg.rs tomorrow, every parity test fails simultaneously with byte-divergent output.


What this means for ML practitioners
ML reproducibility is famously hard. A typical “deterministic” PyTorch model can produce different bytes across runs due to:

CUDA atomic ops (non-deterministic reduction order on GPU)
cuDNN algorithm selection (can pick different kernels per run)
Multi-threaded CPU reductions (thread-scheduling non-determinism)
FMA fusion (compiler-dependent instruction selection)
Hash-randomization in Python’s dict

Each of these has been a real production-incident root cause. ABNG’s parity gates prove the absence of these bugs every test run.


Connection to the reproducibility crisis literature







Source
Claim




Pineau et al. 2021, “Improving Reproducibility in ML Research”
Most ML papers can’t be reproduced even with the same code


Hutson 2018, “Artificial intelligence faces reproducibility crisis”
Structural problem in the field


TensorFlow Determinism Project (tf.config.experimental.enable_op_determinism)
Best-effort, with known gaps


PyTorch determinism guide
Documents environmental flags; doesn’t guarantee bit-equality


ML reproducibility checklists (NeurIPS 2019+)
Process-level requirements


ABNG parity gates
Cryptographic byte-equality across pipelines



ABNG’s contribution to this conversation: byte-equality is achievable if you commit to determinism-by-construction at every layer (no FMA, no random hash, no thread-local RNG). The parity gates prove it run by run.


What this enables

Reproducibility — same seed → same model bytes; papers ship verifiable results.
Cross-platform deployment — train on Linux, deploy on Windows → byte-identical model (modulo cross-platform CI verification, which is the next piece of work).
Distributed training — deterministic operations enable provable consistency without per-step coordination.
Regulatory replay — regulator can re-run training and verify every byte.
CI / regression detection — byte-equal output makes test-failure debugging trivial — diff lines, not “it’s flaky.”
Differential testing across executor versions — new optimizer? Old vs new must produce same bytes for all valid programs.







Warning



Cross-platform determinism is not yet verified. All canary hashes were computed on Windows. They should hold on Linux/macOS (Kahan, no FMA, BTreeMap), but this isn’t yet tested in CI. Adding GitHub Actions CI that runs the suite on three platforms and compares the canaries is a one-day project — and one of the first items on the Phase 0.6 list.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
text:
Phase 0.5 in numbers
The current ABNG state is the result of Phase 0.5, which shipped:







Item
What it added




v12 wire-format bump
Per-node provenance_stamp_hash field + new 0x1C ProvenanceStamped audit kind; NodeStats::canonical_bytes extended 24B → 32B to capture the Kahan compensation register


smart_replay API
Opt-in tamper detection via StatsSnapshot consistency check (cycle-saving fast-forward layer deferred to Phase 0.6)


TOML --config files for cjcl abng train
Hand-rolled minimal TOML parser (zero external deps), full schema for graph / codebook / leaf_head / blr_prior / density / calibration / decision_policy / training / output sections


Chess RL v2.6 retrofit scaffold
Sibling test file demonstrating the API pattern; locked v2.6 weight-hash canary


6 capability demos
OOD detection, adaptive triggers, calibration, drift, compact_log, maturity — all in CJC-Lang source with AST↔︎MIR parity gates


3 application demos
PINN, tabular GP-like, lineage attestation — both Rust-direct AND CJC-Lang versions



Net impact: 906 → 960 tests, +54 in this final round, 0 failures across the whole suite.

title: ABNG — An Experimental Adaptive Belief Network Graph for CJC-Lang
section: What’s next — and why interpreter / compiler work matters
text:
What’s next — and why interpreter / compiler work matters
ABNG is genuinely useful for a specific niche: regulated ML training where you need Bayesian uncertainty + cryptographically-verifiable lineage on tabular data. The demos prove the niche capabilities; they don’t prove ABNG should replace PyTorch.
Three things stand between current ABNG and production readiness:

Cross-platform determinism CI
The locked canary hashes are Windows-only today. They should hold across platforms (the architecture is designed for it), but this is unverified. Adding GitHub Actions CI that runs the full test suite on Linux + macOS + Windows and compares the locked canaries is a one-day project that pays off forever — every future commit either preserves cross-platform determinism or fails CI loudly.


Performance benchmarks at scale
This is the biggest single gap between the current state and production readiness, and it deserves emphasis: none of the demos in this post test ABNG at production-scale data. Sample sizes range from 3 (adaptive triggers) to 200 (tabular GP-like). Every demo runs in milliseconds. That’s an asset for test-suite reproducibility but a liability for the “ABNG works at scale” story.
What’s specifically not yet validated:







Scale claim
Status




“ABNG O(N · d²) beats classical GP O(N³)”
Correct by construction, never wall-clock-benchmarked vs SVGP / sklearn / GPy at n ∈ {10³, 10⁴, 10⁵, 10⁶}


“Per-leaf BLR posterior converges to truth on real PDE problems”
Demonstrated on 1-D heat eq with 40 samples; never on 2-D Burgers / Navier-Stokes at 10⁴ collocation points


“Lineage attestation works on real anonymized clinical-trial data”
Demonstrated on 16-row synthetic; never on a real Phase II/III trial corpus


“Drift detection catches realistic concept drift”
Demonstrated on a controlled mean shift; never on production streaming data


“OOD score generalizes to high-D embeddings”
Demonstrated on 4-D codebook-routed inputs; fundamentally outside scope for unstructured high-D inputs


“Per-leaf calibration scales to real classifiers”
Demonstrated on 30 synthetic (p, y) pairs; never on real classifier outputs at 10³+ predictions



Closing these gaps is the bulk of Phase 0.6 + 0.7 work. Each is a real deliverable: pick a benchmark dataset / workload, write the wall-clock measurement code, run it, ship the numbers. Until that’s done, ABNG is empirically demonstrated at small scale and theoretically expected to hold at larger scale.


Compiler / interpreter performance
This is the bigger long-term constraint. The CJC-Lang interpreter is currently 5–50× slower than direct Rust API access for ABNG demos. For research / experimentation that’s fine. For production ML training at scale, it’s a hard ceiling.
The chess RL benchmark hit this wall directly — the bottleneck for v2.5 was interpreter throughput, not ML algorithm design. Specific levers, in increasing order of effort:

MIR optimizer passes — current optimizer has CF + DCE; further passes (loop-invariant code motion, common subexpression elimination, function inlining) are weeks of work each.
Hot-path specialization in cjc-runtime — ABNG already shows the pattern (75 native builtins for what users actually do). Has architectural cost: every native kernel is a feature you can’t compose out of simpler primitives.
AOT compilation — cjcl compile foo.cjcl → foo.exe. LLVM or Cranelift backend. Months of work but the only path that closes the perf gap meaningfully.

Most likely sequencing: continue ABNG (Phase 0.6 work — smart-replay fast-forward, cross-platform CI, lineage case study) while landing incremental MIR optimizer + native-kernel work. AOT is a multi-month commitment that depends on whether CJC-Lang’s long-term identity is research vehicle or production language.






Note



ABNG lives at crates/cjc-abng/ in the CJC-Lang repository. The 13 demo files are under tests/test_abng_*.rs and tests/abng_demos/. The full test count is 960; the source is open.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
🕑 ~20 min read
I built a chess-playing AI from scratch — in a programming language I also built from scratch. No PyTorch. No NumPy. No Stockfish. Zero external dependencies.
This post is a technical walkthrough of how that works: the language design decisions that made it possible, the RL algorithm, the chess engine, the autodiff engine, the performance engineering, and what I learned along the way.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
The Live Demo
Before diving into the technical details, here’s the training visualization running live. This shows the agent playing self-play chess games, with real-time stats from the v2.5 training run:










Tip



The board animates through sample self-play games. The stats, speed bars, and evolution list are all real numbers from the v2.5 training run.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
What is CJC-Lang?
CJC-Lang (Computational Jacobian Core) is a numerical programming language I wrote in Rust — 21 crates, roughly 96,000 lines of code. It’s designed for ML pipelines, numerical systems, and scientific computing where reproducibility matters.
Reproducibility is a first-class concern, not an afterthought. The language enforces it through specific engineering choices:

Kahan summation for all floating-point reductions — prevents accumulation error from corrupting results
Ordered data structures throughout (BTreeMap/BTreeSet, never HashMap) — no iteration-order non-determinism
Explicit seed threading via SplitMix64 — every source of randomness is traceable
No FMA instructions in SIMD paths — fused multiply-add can produce different results on different hardware
Two execution backends (AST interpreter + MIR register machine) that are required to produce identical results

The result: same input, same output. Every time.
CJC-Lang has built-in tensors, automatic differentiation, copy-on-write memory, pattern matching, closures, structs, for/while loops, if-expressions, file I/O, and SVG visualization. The compiler pipeline looks like this:




flowchart LR
    S["Source\n.cjcl"] --> L["Lexer"] --> P["Parser"] --> A["AST"] --> T["Type\nChecker"] --> H["HIR"] --> M["MIR"] --> E["Executor"]


CJC-Lang compiler pipeline. Source code flows through seven stages before execution.



The chess RL demo exercises nearly every one of these language features — it’s the most demanding program CJC-Lang runs.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
The Autodiff Engine
This is where CJC-Lang’s runtime does the heavy lifting. The RL algorithm above calls grad_graph.backward(loss) — but what happens inside that call?

GradGraph: Arena-Based Automatic Differentiation
CJC-Lang’s autodiff engine is a reverse-mode automatic differentiation system. It builds a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of operations during the forward pass, then traverses it backward to compute gradients.
The original implementation (v2.0) used the standard Rust approach: Vec<Rc<RefCell<GradNode>>> — each node was a reference-counted, interior-mutable object. This works, but every .borrow() call has runtime overhead, and each node allocation hits the heap individually.
In v2.4, the engine was redesigned to use arena allocation: flat arrays for operations, tensors, and gradients. This eliminated roughly 120 .borrow() calls per backward pass.
v2.0: Each node is a separate heap allocation
  [Node 0] → [Node 1] → [Node 2] → ...  (Rc<RefCell<GradNode>> per node)

v2.4+: Flat arena arrays — one contiguous block
  ops:     [Op0, Op1, Op2, Op3, ...]      (Vec<GradOp>)
  tensors: [T0,  T1,  T2,  T3,  ...]      (Vec<Tensor>)
  grads:   [G0,  G1,  G2,  G3,  ...]      (Vec<Option<Tensor>>)


Dead Node Elimination
Not every node in the computation graph is reachable from the loss. In a multi-head architecture (policy + value), the backward pass from the policy loss doesn’t need gradients for nodes only used by the value head (and vice versa during the value backward pass).
Before traversing backward, the engine builds a reachability set from the loss node. Unreachable nodes are skipped entirely — typically 20-30% of the graph for multi-head networks.


In-Place Gradient Accumulation (v2.5)
The original gradient accumulation created a new tensor every time two gradients were added:
// Before: allocates a new tensor
grads[idx] = Some(existing.add_unchecked(&incoming));
In v2.5, this was replaced with in-place mutation:
// After: mutates existing tensor
existing.add_assign_unchecked(&incoming);
This uses Buffer::borrow_data_mut() to get a mutable reference to the underlying data and update it element-wise. The result: roughly half the tensor allocations per backward pass are eliminated. This single change was responsible for the v2.4 → v2.5 speedup (43.5 → 29.8 seconds per episode).


Fused MLP Layers (v2.5)
A standard dense layer in the computation graph creates four separate nodes:

Transpose the weight matrix
Matrix multiply input by transposed weights
Add bias
Apply activation function

The fused GradOp::MlpLayer collapses all four into a single graph node:
MlpLayer {
    input: usize,    // index of input tensor
    weight: usize,   // index of weight tensor
    bias: usize,     // index of bias tensor
    activation: Activation,  // ReLU, Tanh, etc.
}
This reduces graph nodes by 3x per layer and computes all three parameter gradients (d_input, d_weight, d_bias) in a single fused backward pass.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
Performance Engineering

The Profiling-Driven Approach
Every optimization was driven by measurement, not intuition. CJC-Lang has built-in profiling builtins (profile_zone_start, profile_zone_stop, profile_dump) that I used to instrument the training loop.
The first profile revealed the bottleneck distribution:



Function
% of Rollout Time




score_moves (forward pass per move)
84.2%


legal_moves (move generation)
7.7%


apply_move (board update)
0.1%


Everything else
8.0%



84% of time was spent evaluating the neural network for each legal move. The fix: score_moves_batch, a native Rust builtin that evaluates all legal moves in a single batched forward pass instead of looping through them one at a time in the interpreter.
Result: 7.7x rollout speedup.


The Amdahl’s Law Moment
Here’s where it got interesting. A 7.7x speedup on the rollout phase should have been transformative. But total episode time barely moved:
Before: rollout ~10.4s + backward ~25s = ~35s
After:  rollout ~1.3s  + backward ~25s = ~26s
Speedup: ~1.35x (not 7.7x)
Amdahl’s Law in action. The rollout was only about 30% of total episode time. Making it 7.7x faster saved ~9 seconds, but the backward pass (which was untouched) still took 25 seconds. The bottleneck had shifted.
This insight redirected the entire optimization strategy. Instead of making the interpreter faster, I redesigned the autodiff engine:



Version
Change
Episode Time
Speedup




v2.3
Native rollout kernels
82.2 s
baseline


v2.4
Arena GradGraph + dead node elimination
43.5 s
1.89x


v2.5
In-place gradients + fused MLP
29.8 s
2.76x





Native Hot-Path Kernels
Three performance-critical functions were promoted from CJC-Lang code to native Rust builtins:
adam_step — Element-wise Adam optimizer update. The CJC-Lang version was a scalar loop over every parameter; the native version operates directly on tensor buffers. ~9x faster.
encode_state_fast — Board-to-tensor encoding. The CJC-Lang version used array_push to build the 774-element feature vector, which triggered a copy-on-write clone at each push. The native version fills a pre-allocated buffer in a single pass. O(n) vs O(38n).
score_moves_batch — Batched move scoring. Instead of calling the forward pass once per legal move (in the interpreter), this evaluates all moves in a single native call with one matrix multiplication. 7.7x rollout speedup.
All three builtins produce bit-identical output to their CJC-Lang counterparts — verified by parity tests.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
Training Results

Honest Numbers
After 120 episodes of self-play training:

Elo change: +13.9 — the first measurable learning signal in the v2.x series
Win rate vs random: 50% — not yet reliably beating a random opponent
Win rate vs greedy: 50% — ties with a material-greedy baseline
Non-zero terminal rewards: 6/120 episodes — most games are still drawn
