title: Syntax Guide for CJC Version 0.1.1
text:
Part 10 — Design Decisions and Trade-offs

Why No HashMap?
HashMap uses random iteration order in Rust (and most languages). This means any code that iterates over a hash map produces non-deterministic results. CJC-Lang uses BTreeMap and BTreeSet exclusively, trading some average-case performance for guaranteed deterministic iteration.


Why No FMA?
Fused multiply-add (FMA) computes a * b + c in a single instruction with one rounding step instead of two. This is faster and more accurate — but it produces different results depending on whether the hardware has FMA support. CJC-Lang uses mul_fixed (separate multiply and add) to guarantee identical results across all platforms.


Why Two Execution Engines?
The AST interpreter (cjc-eval) is simpler and easier to debug. The MIR executor (cjc-mir-exec) is faster and supports optimization. Having both means:

New features can be prototyped in the interpreter first
Parity tests catch subtle lowering bugs
Users can choose the right trade-off for their workload



Why Vec-Based Pauli Terms?
Quantum Hamiltonians are sums of Pauli terms. A natural representation is a hash map from Pauli string to coefficient. CJC-Lang uses Vec<PauliTerm> instead, because:

Iteration order is deterministic
No hashing overhead for small Hamiltonians
Summation order is fixed, so Kahan compensation works reliably



Why Sign-Stabilized SVD?
SVD is ambiguous in the signs of singular vectors. Two runs of the same SVD can produce vectors that differ by a sign flip. CJC-Lang’s MPS canonical form uses a sign convention (largest-magnitude element is positive) to eliminate this ambiguity. This is critical for deterministic tensor network simulations.

title: Syntax Guide for CJC Version 0.1.1
text:
What’s Next
CJC-Lang v0.1.1 lays the groundwork for quantum-classical hybrid algorithms, noise-aware quantum simulation, and tensor network methods. Upcoming work includes:

Multi-file module system — programs that span multiple files with deterministic resolution
Default parameters and variadic functions — fn solve(x: f64, tol: f64 = 1e-6)
Decorator syntax — @log @timed fn train(x: Tensor)
Sparse eigensolvers — Lanczos and Arnoldi with deterministic iteration
MIR autodiff integration — gradients flowing through the MIR optimization pipeline

The language is growing, but the invariants remain fixed: no hidden allocations, no non-deterministic operations, no silent precision loss. Same seed, same bits, always.







CJC-Lang is open source and under active development. The codebase spans 21 Rust crates with zero external runtime dependencies. If deterministic numerical computing interests you, check out the introductory post or the v0.1.0 release notes.

title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
text: 🕑 ~20 min read

title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
text:
What Is cjcl?
cjcl is the CLI frontend for CJC-Lang, a deterministic numerical programming language. One binary, zero external dependencies, 30 commands that span compilation, data engineering, profiling, and reproducibility verification.
cargo install cjc-lang
cjcl --version   # CJC-Lang v0.1.6
Every command that produces output supports three output modes: --color (default, human-readable), --plain (no ANSI escapes, pipe-friendly), and --json (machine-readable). Most commands that execute code accept --seed <N> for deterministic RNG seeding (default: 42).

title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
text:
Quick Reference







Category
Commands




Core Compiler
lex, parse, check, run, eval, repl


Data & Pipeline
view, proof, flow, patch, seek, drift, forge


Inspection & Diagnostics
inspect, schema, trace, mem, bench, pack, doctor


Compiler Visibility
emit, explain, gc, nogc, audit, precision, lock, parity, test, ci

title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
text:
Core Compiler Commands
These six commands are the foundation — tokenize, parse, type-check, run, evaluate, and interact.

lex
Tokenize a source file and print the token stream.
cjcl lex program.cjcl
Output shows each token’s span, kind, and text. Useful for debugging parser issues or understanding how CJC-Lang sees your source code.


parse
Parse a source file and pretty-print the AST.
cjcl parse program.cjcl
Shows the full abstract syntax tree after parsing. Use this when you suspect a precedence or grouping issue — the AST reveals exactly how the parser interpreted your expressions.


check
Type-check a source file without running it.
cjcl check program.cjcl
Runs the full compilation pipeline (lex, parse, type-check) and reports errors and warnings. Exit code 0 means the program is well-typed.
When used with validation flags, check becomes an output validator:
cjcl check output.csv --against expected.csv --tolerance 1e-6
cjcl check output.csv --against expected.csv --schema-only
cjcl check output.txt --expect "Hello, world!"



Flag
What it does




--against <file>
Compare file against a reference


--tolerance <N>
Numeric tolerance for comparison (default: 0.0)


--schema-only
Compare only column structure, not values


--expect <string>
Check file contents match a literal string



When to use it: Run cjcl check before committing to catch type errors early. Use the validation mode in CI to verify pipeline outputs haven’t drifted.


run
Execute a CJC-Lang program.
cjcl run program.cjcl
cjcl run program.cjcl --seed 123
cjcl run program.cjcl --mir-opt
cjcl run program.cjcl --multi-file
This is the command you’ll use most. By default it uses the AST tree-walk interpreter. Add --mir-opt to use the MIR register-machine executor with optimizations (control flow simplification + dead code elimination).



Flag
What it does




--seed <N>
RNG seed (default: 42)


--mir-opt
MIR executor with optimizations


--mir-mono
MIR executor with monomorphization


--multi-file
Enable multi-file module resolution


--time
Print execution time to stderr


--format json
Wrap output in {"ok":true,"output":[...]}



Exit codes: 0 = success, 1 = runtime error, 2 = parse error, 3 = type error, 4 = parity failure.
When to use it: This is your daily driver. Use --mir-opt for performance-sensitive code, --multi-file for projects split across modules.


eval
Evaluate a single expression without creating a file.
cjcl eval "42 + 8"           # → 50
cjcl eval "sqrt(2.0)"        # → 1.4142135623730951
cjcl eval "42 + 8" --format json  # → {"ok":true,"output":["50"]}
The expression is wrapped in fn main() { print(<expr>); } and executed. Supports --seed and --format.
When to use it: Quick calculations, testing builtins, scripting one-liners.


repl
Start an interactive read-eval-print loop.
cjcl repl
cjcl repl --seed 123
The REPL preserves variable bindings across lines and supports meta-commands:



Command
What it does




:help
Show all meta-commands


:type <expr>
Show inferred type


:ast <expr>
Pretty-print the AST


:mir <expr>
Show MIR representation


:env
Show current variable bindings


:time <expr>
Time expression execution


:describe <expr>
Statistical summary of a numeric array


:save <file>
Save session history


:load <file>
Load and execute a file


:reset
Clear all bindings


:seed
Show current RNG seed


:quit
Exit



When to use it: Exploratory data analysis, testing ideas, learning the language.

title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
text:
Data & Pipeline Commands
Seven commands for working with data files, verifying reproducibility, and building deterministic pipelines.

view
Deterministic directory listing with type-aware annotations.
cjcl view
cjcl view ./data/ --recursive --hash
Lists files sorted lexicographically with color-coded types: .cjcl files show effect annotations (#[nogc], @pure, @timed), .snap files show content hashes, data files (.csv, .json) are highlighted. Output is deterministic — same directory, same listing, every time.



Flag
What it does




-r, --recursive
Recurse into subdirectories


--hash
Show SHA-256 hashes


--no-effects
Hide effect annotations


--no-size
Hide file sizes



When to use it: Quickly inventory a project’s data files and verify effect annotations.


proof
Verify that a program produces identical output across multiple runs.
cjcl proof program.cjcl
cjcl proof program.cjcl --runs 10 --seeds 42,123,999
cjcl proof program.cjcl --executor both --hash-output
Runs the program N times (default: 3) and checks that stdout, exit status, and GC behavior are identical. With --seeds, tests multiple seeds. With --executor both, verifies both the AST interpreter and MIR executor.



Flag
What it does




-n, --runs <N>
Number of iterations (default: 3)


--seed <N>
Single seed to test (default: 42)


--seeds <N,M,...>
Test multiple seeds


--executor eval\|mir\|both
Which executor(s) to use


--fail-fast
Stop on first divergence


--hash-output
Show SHA-256 of stdout per run


--save-report <file>
Save report as JSON



When to use it: Before publishing results. Before merging a PR that touches numerical code. In CI as a gate.


flow
Streaming computation over CSV/TSV/JSONL data with O(ncols) memory.
cjcl flow sales.csv --op sum,mean,min,max
cjcl flow sales.csv --op mean --columns revenue,cost --sort-by mean
cjcl flow --tsv < data.tsv --top 5 --json
Processes data in a single pass without loading the entire file into memory. Uses Kahan summation for numeric stability and Welford’s online algorithm for variance/stddev.







Flag
What it does




--op <ops>
Operations: sum, mean, min, max, count, var, std


-c, --columns <cols>
Only process named columns


--sort-by <metric>
Sort output by a metric


--top <N>
Show only top N columns


--verify
Run twice, confirm identical output


--tsv
Tab-delimited input


-d, --delimiter <c>
Custom delimiter



When to use it: Quick summary statistics on large CSVs without loading into a DataFrame. Verifying that a pipeline’s output statistics match expectations.


patch
Type-aware data transformation for CSV/TSV/JSONL files.
cjcl patch data.csv --nan-fill 0.0
cjcl patch data.csv --impute salary --drop temp_col --rename old_name new_name
cjcl patch data.csv --replace status "NA" "unknown" --plan
Streams the file and applies transformations: NaN replacement, mean imputation, column dropping, renaming, value replacement. With --plan, shows the transformation sequence without executing. With --dry-run, previews changes.



Flag
What it does




--nan-fill <value>
Replace NaN/NA/null with a value


--impute <column>
Mean imputation for a column


--drop <column>
Drop a column


--rename <from> <to>
Rename a column


--replace <col> <from> <to>
Replace exact values


--fill-empty <col> <value>
Fill empty cells


--plan
Show transformation plan


--dry-run
Preview without writing


--in-place
Modify file directly


-o, --output <file>
Output file (default: stdout)



When to use it: Quick data cleaning before feeding into a CJC-Lang pipeline. Fixing nulls and renaming columns without writing a script.


seek
Deterministic file discovery with content search.
cjcl seek . "*.cjcl"
cjcl seek ./data --type csv --contains "revenue" --hash
cjcl seek . --min-size 1MB --sort size --manifest
Recursively finds files matching patterns with stable, sorted output. With --contains, searches file contents. With --manifest, outputs <hash> <size> <path> per line — useful for reproducibility manifests.



Flag
What it does




-t, --type <type>
Filter by extension (cjcl, csv, snap, etc.)


--contains <pattern>
Content search


--min-size <size>
Minimum file size (supports KB/MB)


--max-size <size>
Maximum file size


--max-depth <N>
Recursion depth limit


--hash
Show SHA-256 per file


--manifest
Output hash, size, path per line


--sort name\|size\|modified
Sort mode


--first <N>
Limit results


--ignore-build-artifacts
Skip target/, node_modules/, etc.



When to use it: Building reproducibility manifests. Finding all data files in a project. Content search across a codebase.


drift
Compare two files with mathematical precision.
cjcl drift output_v1.csv output_v2.csv --tolerance 1e-6
cjcl drift model_a.csv model_b.csv --stats-only --json
cjcl drift old.txt new.txt --text --fail-on-diff
Supports text (line-by-line), numeric (element-wise), CSV (cell-by-cell), and JSONL diff modes. Reports max deviation, mean deviation, Frobenius norm, and per-cell differences.







Flag
What it does




--tolerance <N>
Numeric tolerance (default: 0.0)


--text, --numeric, --csv, --jsonl
Diff mode (auto-detected from extension)


--max-diffs <N>
Maximum differences to show (default: 50)


--fail-on-diff
Exit 1 if any differences found


--fail-on-schema-diff
Exit 1 if column names differ


--summary-only
Only show summary metrics


--stats-only
Only show statistical metrics


--report <file>
Write full diff report to JSON



When to use it: Comparing model outputs between versions. Verifying that a code change didn’t alter numerical results. Regression testing in CI.


forge
Content-addressable pipeline runner.
cjcl forge run pipeline.cjcl
cjcl forge verify pipeline.cjcl abc123def
cjcl forge list
cjcl forge show abc123
cjcl forge clean
Executes a program, captures its output, and stores the result as a SHA-256 content-addressable artifact. With verify, checks that the current output matches an expected hash. Artifacts are stored in .cjc-forge/.



Subcommand
What it does




run <file>
Execute and store artifact


verify <file> <hash>
Verify output matches hash


list
List cached artifacts


show <hash-prefix>
Show artifact details


clean
Remove all cached artifacts






Flag
What it does




--seed <N>
RNG seed (default: 42)


--cache-dir <path>
Cache directory (default: .cjc-forge/)



When to use it: Pinning pipeline outputs for reproducibility. Verifying that a computation produces the exact same output on a different machine.

title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
text:
Inspection & Diagnostics
Seven commands for understanding what your code does, how fast it runs, and whether your project is healthy.

inspect
Deep inspection of structured files without execution.
cjcl inspect data.csv
cjcl inspect model.snap --deep
cjcl inspect results.csv --compare expected.csv
cjcl inspect data.parquet --schema-only
Inspects .snap, CSV/TSV, JSONL, .cjcl, Parquet, Arrow, SQLite, and model files. Reports structure, shape, column types, null counts, and basic statistics. With --deep, computes variance, standard deviation, and unique value counts.



Flag
What it does




--deep
Compute variance, unique values


--preview <N>
Max preview lines (default: 5)


--header-only
Show header/schema only


--compare <file>
Compare schemas/hashes with another file


--hash
Show SHA-256 hash


--manifest
Output manifest format



When to use it: Understanding unfamiliar datasets. Verifying schema before piping into a pipeline. Quick sanity checks on model checkpoints.


schema
Infer and validate data schemas.
cjcl schema data.csv
cjcl schema data.csv --full --save schema.json
cjcl schema data.csv --check schema.json
cjcl schema data.csv --diff old_schema.json
Infers column types (null, bool, int, float, string, mixed), null counts, and unique value counts from CSV/TSV/JSONL/Parquet/Arrow/SQLite data. With --save, persists the schema. With --check, validates data against a saved schema. With --diff, shows schema drift.







Flag
What it does




--full
Show type distributions and sample values


--save <file>
Save schema to JSON


--check <file>
Validate against saved schema (exit 1 on mismatch)


--diff <file>
Compare current vs saved schema


--sample <N>
Limit to first N rows


--strict
Treat warnings as errors



When to use it: Detecting schema drift between pipeline runs. Validating incoming data before processing. Documenting dataset structure.


trace
Execution tracing across four phases: parse, static analysis, execution, and reporting.
cjcl trace program.cjcl
cjcl trace program.cjcl --ast --verbose
Reports parse time, execution time, AST structure (function/struct/statement counts), effect annotations, and runtime behavior (output lines, GC collections).



Flag
What it does




--ast
Show AST details


--no-output
Suppress program output


-v, --verbose
Show function list



When to use it: Understanding the structure of a program. Getting a quick overview of parse and execution cost.


mem
Runtime memory profiling.
cjcl mem program.cjcl
cjcl mem program.cjcl --runs 5 --timeline --compare eval|mir
Executes a program and reports GC collection counts, heap object counts, output size, and execution time. With --runs, aggregates statistics across multiple executions. With --compare, runs both executors and shows side-by-side GC behavior.



Flag
What it does




-n, --runs <N>
Number of runs (default: 1)


--nogc
Run NoGC verification first


--eval / --mir
Choose executor


--peak-only
Only report peak metrics


--timeline
Show GC collections per run


--compare eval\|mir
Compare executor GC behavior


--fail-on-gc
Exit 1 if any GC collections occurred


--save-report <file>
Save report as JSON



When to use it: Identifying GC pressure. Verifying that a #[nogc] function truly avoids allocations. Comparing memory behavior between executors.


bench
Performance benchmarking with statistical rigor.
cjcl bench program.cjcl --runs 20 --warmup 5
cjcl bench program.cjcl --compare eval|mir --markdown
cjcl bench program.cjcl --baseline baseline.json --fail-if-slower-than 10
Runs the program N times with warmup iterations and reports mean, median, min, max, stddev, coefficient of variation, P95, and throughput.



Flag
What it does




-n, --runs <N>
Measured runs (default: 10)


--warmup <N>
Warmup runs (default: 1)


--mir / --eval
Choose executor


--compare eval\|mir
Side-by-side executor comparison


--baseline <file>
Load baseline for comparison


--save-baseline <file>
Save results as baseline


--fail-if-slower-than <N>
Exit 1 if >N% slower than baseline


--csv / --markdown
Output as CSV or Markdown table


-v, --verbose
Show per-run times



When to use it: Measuring performance before and after a change. Comparing executor backends. Setting up CI performance gates.


pack
Bundle a program and its dependencies into a reproducible package.
cjcl pack pipeline.cjcl
cjcl pack pipeline.cjcl --include extra_data.csv --verify
cjcl pack --list pipeline.pack
cjcl pack --repro-check pipeline.pack
Auto-discovers dependencies by scanning source for string literals matching packable extensions (.snap, .csv, .tsv, .json, .lock, .toml). Creates a directory with a MANIFEST containing per-file SHA-256 hashes.



Flag
What it does




-o, --output <dir>
Output directory


--include <file>
Additional file to include (repeatable)


--verify
Verify manifest after packing


--dry-run
Preview without writing


--list <dir.pack>
List package contents


--repro-check <dir>
Re-hash and verify against manifest


--manifest-only
Output MANIFEST only



When to use it: Sharing reproducible computations. Archiving pipeline artifacts. Verifying that a package hasn’t been tampered with.


doctor
Project-wide diagnostics.
cjcl doctor
cjcl doctor ./my_project --fix --dry-run
cjcl doctor --strict --report findings.json
Scans a directory for: parse errors, type errors, corrupt .snap files, ragged CSV rows, nondeterministic patterns (HashMap, HashSet, rand(), time()), large files, malformed JSONL, and invalid Parquet files.



Flag
What it does




--fix
Attempt automatic fixes


--dry-run
Preview fixes without applying


--strict
Exit 1 on warnings


--category <type>
Filter by finding category


--report <file>
Save findings to JSON


--summary-only
Only show summary counts


--fail-on <level>
Exit 1 at severity threshold



When to use it: Pre-commit health check. CI gate for project hygiene. Onboarding — run doctor to understand a new project’s state.

title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
text:
Compiler Visibility & Analysis
Ten commands that expose the compiler’s internals — intermediate representations, memory verification, numerical analysis, and dual-executor parity.

emit
Dump intermediate representations at any pipeline stage.
cjcl emit program.cjcl --stage ast
cjcl emit program.cjcl --stage hir
cjcl emit program.cjcl --stage mir
cjcl emit program.cjcl --stage mir --opt --diff



Flag
What it does




-s, --stage ast\|hir\|mir
Pipeline stage (default: mir)


--opt
Show optimized MIR


--diff
Show both unoptimized and optimized MIR



When to use it: Understanding what the compiler does to your code. Debugging unexpected behavior by inspecting the IR. Verifying that optimizations preserve semantics.


explain
Show the desugared form of a program after HIR lowering.
cjcl explain program.cjcl
cjcl explain program.cjcl --verbose
Shows function signatures, effect annotations, struct definitions, and a summary table. With --verbose, shows full HIR bodies. Useful for understanding what syntactic sugar (for-loops, match expressions, closures) compiles down to.


gc
Dedicated GC analysis with allocation timeline.
cjcl gc program.cjcl --runs 5 --verbose
Executes the program multiple times and reports GC pause count, collections per run, heap object peaks, COW copies, and execution time. Shows whether GC behavior is stable across runs.



Flag
What it does




-n, --runs <N>
Number of runs (default: 3)


-v, --verbose
Show allocation timeline



When to use it: Identifying allocation hotspots. Verifying GC stability for determinism.


nogc
Static verification that #[nogc] functions don’t allocate.
cjcl nogc program.cjcl
cjcl nogc program.cjcl --verbose
Lists all functions with their #[nogc] annotation status and runs the NoGC verifier. Reports per-function PASS/FAIL with violation details. Exit code 1 if any violations found.
When to use it: Verifying that performance-critical functions avoid GC. Required for functions that will run in the NoGC execution mode.


audit
Static analysis for floating-point anti-patterns.
cjcl audit program.cjcl
cjcl audit program.cjcl --strict --category summation
cjcl audit program.cjcl --baseline baseline.json --report findings.json
Detects: naive summation in loops (suggests kahan_sum()), float equality comparisons (suggests approx_eq()), unguarded division, and potential catastrophic cancellation. No execution required — purely static analysis.







Flag
What it does




--strict
Treat INFO as warnings, warnings as errors


--category <type>
Filter: summation, float-eq, division, cancellation


--baseline <file>
Only report new findings


--report <file>
Save findings as JSON


--suggest-only
Only show suggestions



When to use it: Code review for numerical code. CI gate for floating-point hygiene. Onboarding — audit unfamiliar numerical code before trusting its output.


precision
Analyze whether f64 precision matters for your output.
cjcl precision program.cjcl
cjcl precision program.cjcl --epsilon 1e-10 --fail-on-instability
Executes the program, extracts numeric values from output, compares f64 against f32-truncated values, and reports relative error per value. Verdict: STABLE (within epsilon) or SENSITIVE (exceeds epsilon).







Flag
What it does




--epsilon <N>
Comparison threshold (default: ~1.19e-7, f32 epsilon)


--fail-on-instability
Exit 1 if any value exceeds epsilon


--summary-only
Only show pass/fail


--report <file>
Save report as JSON



When to use it: Deciding whether f32 is sufficient for a workload. Identifying precision-sensitive computations. CI gate for numerical stability.


lock
Generate and verify deterministic lockfiles.
cjcl lock program.cjcl
cjcl lock program.cjcl --verify
cjcl lock program.cjcl --show
cjcl lock program.cjcl --diff
cjcl lock program.cjcl --executor mir --update
Creates a lockfile containing: source SHA-256 hash, seed, executor version, platform, and expected output SHA-256 hash. With --verify, checks that the current execution matches the recorded lockfile.



Flag
What it does




--verify
Verify current run against lockfile


--update
Regenerate lockfile


--show
Display lockfile contents


--diff
Show field-by-field differences


--executor eval\|mir
Which executor (default: eval)



When to use it: Pinning expected output for CI. Detecting when a code or platform change alters results. Cross-platform reproducibility verification.


parity
Verify that both executors produce identical results.
cjcl parity program.cjcl
cjcl parity program.cjcl --verbose --explain-mismatch
cjcl parity program.cjcl --function my_function --save-report parity.json
Runs both the AST interpreter (v1) and MIR executor (v2) with the same seed and compares stdout, exit status, return value, and GC collections.



Flag
What it does




-v, --verbose
Show output from both executors


--explain-mismatch
Show first divergent line with context


--function <name>
Only test a specific function


--save-report <file>
Save report as JSON



When to use it: After implementing a new language feature. Before shipping a compiler change. CI gate for executor equivalence.


test
Native test runner for CJC-Lang test functions.
cjcl test program.cjcl
cjcl test program.cjcl --filter "matrix"
cjcl test program.cjcl --verbose
Discovers functions whose name starts with test_ (or decorated with @test), runs them in alphabetical order with a fresh interpreter per test, and reports pass/fail with timing.



Flag
What it does




--filter <pattern>
Only run matching tests


-v, --verbose
Show test output and errors



When to use it: Running tests written in CJC-Lang itself. Quick smoke tests without a Rust test harness.


ci
Full CI diagnostic suite — runs five checks in sequence.
cjcl ci
cjcl ci ./my_project --strict --json
Orchestrates: doctor (parse/type errors) → proof (determinism) → parity (dual-executor) → test (test functions) → nogc (static verification). Reports per-step pass/fail.



Flag
What it does




--seed <N>
Base RNG seed (default: 42)


--strict
Exit 1 on warnings



When to use it: One command in your CI pipeline that checks everything. The nuclear option for project health.

title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
text:
Putting It Together
The real power of the CLI is in composition. Here are four workflows I use regularly:

“I just wrote new code”
cjcl check program.cjcl          # Type errors?
cjcl run program.cjcl             # Does it work?
cjcl parity program.cjcl          # Same result on both executors?


“My pipeline is slow”
cjcl trace program.cjcl           # Where does time go?
cjcl mem program.cjcl --timeline  # GC pressure?
cjcl bench program.cjcl --runs 20 --compare eval|mir  # Which executor is faster?


“I’m shipping a reproducible artifact”
cjcl lock program.cjcl            # Pin expected output
cjcl pack program.cjcl --verify   # Bundle with manifest
cjcl proof program.cjcl --runs 10 --seeds 42,123,999  # Determinism across seeds


“The numbers don’t look right”
cjcl audit program.cjcl           # Float anti-patterns?
cjcl precision program.cjcl       # f64 vs f32 sensitivity?
cjcl drift output_v1.csv output_v2.csv --tolerance 1e-6  # What changed?

title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
text:
Global Flags
These flags work with any command:



Flag
What it does




--seed <N>
RNG seed (default: 42)


--reproducible
Enable reproducibility mode


--time
Print execution time to stderr


--color / --no-color
Control ANSI color output


--diagnostic-format rich\|short
Error display format


--help, -h
Print help


--version, -V
Print version









CJC-Lang is open source and available on crates.io. All 30 commands are included in the single cjcl binary. Run cjcl <command> --help for command-specific help.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
I didn’t set out to build a programming language. I set out to solve a problem.
I spent years working as a lab engineer running experiments on audio devices — collecting data, cleaning it, troubleshooting when results didn’t look right. Was the anomaly real, or was it an environment issue? A script issue? A sensor calibration drift? I learned firsthand how messy data — or messy handling of data — introduces problems that cascade through every stage of analysis. A mistake in the initial data cleaning phase doesn’t just cost time. It costs weeks of downstream work, delays shipping, and erodes confidence in the results.
That experience stuck with me as I moved into data science. In most scientific computing environments, running the same program twice can produce slightly different results. Floating-point reduction order changes between runs. Hash maps iterate in random order. Thread scheduling affects accumulation. RNG state leaks across boundaries. These aren’t bugs — they’re properties of systems that weren’t designed with determinism as a constraint.
I wanted a system where the same program with the same seed produces bit-identical output every time, on every platform. Not approximately the same — exactly the same.
That constraint turned out to touch everything: memory layout, collection types, floating-point arithmetic, random number generation, parallelism. The deeper I went, the clearer it became that bolting determinism onto an existing language would mean fighting the language at every turn. So I built one where determinism is structural.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
What Is CJC-Lang?
CJC-Lang — Computation Jacobian Core — is a programming language I’m building from scratch for deterministic scientific computing. It’s written in Rust, organized as a 20-crate workspace, with nearly all functionality implemented from scratch using only the Rust standard library.






CJC-Lang is currently in beta. The core compiler pipeline and runtime are functional and backed by 5,300+ passing tests, but the language is still under active development and is not production-ready. I’m sharing it now because the ideas behind it are worth discussing even before everything is polished.





20Rust crates


6.4 MBsingle binary


~300built-in functions


5,300+tests passing


The language covers the full compilation and execution pipeline: a lexer, recursive descent parser, type checker, two intermediate representations (HIR and MIR), an optimizer, two execution engines, and a runtime with ~300 built-in functions for math, statistics, linear algebra, machine learning, signal processing, and data manipulation.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Core Ideas

Determinism as Architecture
CJC-Lang enforces determinism at every layer:

Ordered collections everywhere. BTreeMap and BTreeSet instead of hash-based containers. Iteration order is always defined.
Stable floating-point reductions. All summations use Kahan or binned accumulators — order-invariant superaccumulators that produce identical results regardless of evaluation order.
No FMA in SIMD kernels. Fused multiply-add produces different rounding than separate multiply and add. CJC-Lang avoids FMA to keep results identical across CPU architectures.
Seeded, explicit RNG. The random number generator is SplitMix64 with explicit seed threading. No global state, no entropy sources.
Deterministic parallelism. Parallel operations are structured so that results don’t depend on thread count or scheduling.

This isn’t a mode you opt into. It’s how the language works. The determinism guarantee is verified in the test suite — for example, the SciML tests train a physics-informed model twice with the same seed and assert that every parameter is bit-identical:
// From tests/sciml_determinism.rs
let r1 = piml_heat_1d_train(6, 20, 30, 0.01, 1000, 1e-3, 1.0, 10.0, 42);
let r2 = piml_heat_1d_train(6, 20, 30, 0.01, 1000, 1e-3, 1.0, 10.0, 42);
for (i, (&a, &b)) in r1.final_params.iter()
    .zip(r2.final_params.iter()).enumerate()
{
    assert_eq!(a.to_bits(), b.to_bits(),
        "Param {} not bit-identical", i);
}
Not approximately equal. Bit-identical. to_bits() compares the raw IEEE 754 representation.


Nearly Zero External Dependencies
The entire system compiles to a 6.4 MB single binary. That one file contains everything: lexer, parser, type checker, two execution engines, optimizer, ~300 builtins, tensor library, linear algebra kernels, regex engine, AD engine, data manipulation DSL, serialization, visualization, and 15+ CLI subcommands.1
This is a deliberate choice. External dependencies introduce version churn, platform variance, and complexity that’s hard to audit. For a system that promises bit-identical results, every component needs to be under direct control.


Dual Execution Engines
CJC-Lang has two execution paths:

AST interpreter (cjc-eval): walks the syntax tree directly. Simple, easy to debug, good for prototyping.
MIR executor (cjc-mir-exec): lowers through HIR → MIR, applies optimization passes (constant folding, dead code elimination), and runs on a register machine.

Both engines must produce identical results for every program. Parity tests enforce this — if the engines disagree, that’s a bug, and the test suite catches it.


NoGC Verification
CJC-Lang includes a static verifier that can prove code paths are free of GC allocations. The NoGC verifier performs transitive call-graph analysis to ensure that annotated functions — and everything they call — operate within reference-counted, copy-on-write memory without triggering GC. This matters for latency-sensitive workloads where GC pauses are unacceptable.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Beyond Neural Networks
CJC-Lang isn’t just a machine learning language. The numerical infrastructure supports a broader range of scientific computing — and this is where the determinism guarantee pays the biggest dividends. When you’re debugging a failed ODE integration or a clustering result that doesn’t match your colleague’s run, knowing the computation is reproducible eliminates an entire class of “was it the code or was it the environment?” questions.







Domain
Key Capabilities




ODE/PDE Solvers
Euler, RK4, adaptive RK45, 1D Laplacian, diffusion stepping, adjoint sensitivity


Optimization
Gradient descent, BFGS, L-BFGS, Nelder-Mead, bisection, Brent, Newton, secant


Regression
OLS, weighted least squares, logistic regression, polynomial fitting


Clustering
K-means (k-means++ init), DBSCAN, agglomerative (4 linkage methods), PCA


Time Series
ACF, PACF, EWMA, seasonal decomposition, stationarity tests (ADF, KPSS, Phillips-Perron)


Interpolation
Cubic natural splines, linear, nearest-neighbor, Horner polynomial evaluation




Adaptive RK45 integration with step size control

let result = ode_solve_rk45(
    |t: f64, y: f64| -0.5 * y,   // dy/dt = -0.5y (exponential decay)
    0.0,                           // t_start
    10.0,                          // t_end
    1.0,                           // y_initial
    1e-6                           // tolerance
);
print("Final y:", result);         // ~0.00674 (matches exp(-5))

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
section: What’s Native — and Why That Matters
text:
What’s Native — and Why That Matters
Everything mentioned above runs inside a single binary with nearly zero external dependencies. Every component is under direct control, which means determinism guarantees extend through the entire stack — from CSV parsing through model training through serialization.








Component
What It Does
Why Native Matters




Regex (cjc-regex)
Thompson NFA engine — O(n) linear time, no backtracking
Eliminates ReDoS vulnerabilities and supply chain risk


RNG (SplitMix64)
Seeded, explicit state, fork() for parallel streams
Same seed = same output on every platform


Visualization (cjc-vizor)
Grammar-of-graphics with SVG/BMP output
No subprocess, no JS engine, no network calls


DataFrame (cjc-data)
73+ tidyverse-style ops, lazy eval, typed columns
Grouping and aggregation inherit determinism automatically


Serialization (cjc-snap)
Canonical binary format with SHA-256 content hashing
Checkpoints are exact; snap_hash() enables verification across runs


Accumulators (cjc-repro)
Kahan compensated and binned superaccumulators
Order-invariant reductions — the mechanism behind bit-identical floating-point results



No pip install. No npm install. No version conflicts. Just the binary, the source file, and the seed.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
The CLI Toolchain
CJC-Lang ships with 15+ CLI subcommands:
cjcl run program.cjcl              # execute a program
cjcl run program.cjcl --seed 42    # explicit RNG seed
cjcl run program.cjcl --mir-opt    # MIR-optimized execution
cjcl check program.cjcl            # type-check without running
cjcl repl                         # interactive REPL
cjcl trace program.cjcl            # execution tracing
cjcl bench program.cjcl            # benchmarking
cjcl doctor                       # health/validation checks
Additional commands include inspect, schema, flow (control flow graphs), drift (determinism drift detection), mem (memory profiling), pack (binary serialization), and forge (code generation).

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
section: Why CJC-Lang Is Still in Beta
text:
Why CJC-Lang Is Still in Beta
The core is solid: the compiler pipeline works end-to-end, both execution engines agree, the test suite is extensive, and the runtime covers a wide surface area. But several areas need more work:

Type inference is limited — most types require explicit annotation
Generics are parsed but not fully enforced at the type level
The module system exists but needs refinement for multi-file projects
REPL lacks readline support
Optimizer has room for more passes beyond constant folding and DCE
Documentation is sparse outside of test coverage

I’m not rushing to call this production-ready. The point is to build something correct first, then make it convenient.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
What Comes Next
The near-term roadmap includes:

If-expressions: converting if from a statement to an expression (let x = if cond { a } else { b })
Default parameters and variadic functions: making function signatures more flexible
MIR-integrated autodiff: ensuring gradient computation works through the optimized execution path
Module system improvements: reliable multi-file program support
Sparse linear algebra: eigenvalue solvers (Lanczos, Arnoldi) with deterministic iteration

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Why Share This Now?
CJC-Lang isn’t finished, but the ideas behind it are clear enough to discuss. Deterministic scientific computing is a real problem — I’ve seen it firsthand, from the lab bench to the data pipeline. Building the entire stack from scratch is an unusual approach, but the results so far suggest it’s viable.
If you’re interested in compiler design, numerical methods, reproducible computing, or just the experience of building a language from the ground up, I’ll be writing more here as the project develops.
Next up: I’ll walk through the neural network architectures I’ve built and verified in CJC-Lang — REINFORCE agents, physics-informed networks, CNNs, and attention mechanisms, all running deterministically without any ML framework.






Follow the project



CJC-Lang is open source and under active development. The project is in beta — functional and tested, but not yet production-ready. Follow @adamezzat for updates as new features land.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Footnotes


The only external dependencies are in the optional language server (cjc-analyzer) and the rayon crate for parallel tensor operations. The core 18 crates have zero external dependencies.↩︎

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
🕑 ~15 min read
In the previous post, I introduced CJC-Lang and the design decisions behind its determinism guarantees. This post goes deeper into one specific question: can you build real neural network architectures in a language with no ML framework?
The answer is yes — and the determinism makes verification straightforward. Every architecture below has been implemented in pure CJC-Lang, run through both execution engines, and verified with assertions. Same seed, same output, every time.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN)
CJC-Lang’s AD engine supports PINNs — neural networks that learn to satisfy differential equations, not just fit data. This is where determinism isn’t just convenient — it’s scientifically necessary.




flowchart TB
    X["Position x"] --> N["MLP [1 → 32 → 32 → 1]\ntanh activation"] --> U["Prediction u_hat"]
    U --> DL["Data Loss\nMSE"]
    U --> PL["Physics Loss\nu_xx + u ≈ 0"]
    U --> BL["Boundary Loss\nu(0)=0, u(pi)=0"]
    DL --> T["Total Loss"]
    PL --> T
    BL --> T
    T -.->|"Adam optimizer"| N


PINN for the harmonic oscillator. The composite loss combines data fitting, PDE residual, and boundary conditions.



The harmonic oscillator example uses a [1, 32, 32, 1] MLP with tanh activation trained to solve u_xx + u = 0:

Data loss: MSE against observed points (optionally noisy)
Physics loss: the PDE residual u_xx + u computed via finite differences through the network, penalized toward zero
Boundary loss: u(0) and u(π) enforced to match known boundary conditions
Optimizer: Adam with learning rate scheduling

The exact solution is sin(x), and the trained network converges to it with verified L2 error below tolerance.
When you’re claiming a network satisfies a PDE, you need to reproduce the training run exactly to verify the claim. CJC-Lang’s deterministic AD engine, seeded RNG, and Kahan-summed loss computations mean the verification is airtight. The test suite trains the same PINN twice with seed 42 and asserts that every parameter is bit-identical using to_bits() comparison on the raw IEEE 754 representation. No other ML framework provides this guarantee without significant configuration overhead.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Physics-Informed ML (PIML)
A simpler but equally rigorous approach: polynomial regression with a physics residual penalty, applied to the 1D heat equation u_xx = f(x).
The model learns polynomial coefficients that simultaneously fit noisy observations and satisfy the PDE. Boundary conditions are enforced through weighted penalty terms. The exact solution is sin(πx), and the optimized polynomial converges to it.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)
CJC-Lang’s tensor runtime includes native 1D and 2D convolution, maxpooling, and the full pipeline for building convolutional architectures.




flowchart LR
    I["Input\n[4,3,28,28]"] --> C["Conv2D\n32 filters 3x3"] --> R["ReLU"] --> BN["BatchNorm"] --> MP["MaxPool2D\n2x2"] --> O["Output\n[4,32,13,13]"]


CNN pipeline in CJC-Lang. Native conv2d, ReLU, batch normalization, and maxpooling — all deterministic.



// 2D convolution: batch of images through learned filters
let input = Tensor.randn([4, 3, 28, 28]);   // batch=4, channels=3, 28x28
let filters = Tensor.randn([32, 3, 3, 3]);  // 32 output channels, 3x3 kernel
let bias = Tensor.zeros([32]);
let conv_out = input.conv2d(filters, bias, 1);   // -> [4, 32, 26, 26]

// Activation + normalization + pooling
let activated = conv_out.relu();
let pooled = activated.maxpool2d(2, 2);          // -> [4, 32, 13, 13]
CJC-Lang also supports 1D convolution with circular padding (conv1d_circular) for signal processing, and the full set of CNN building blocks: batch_norm, dropout_mask, arbitrary strides, and multi-channel operations.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Attention Mechanism (Transformer Building Block)
Scaled dot-product attention — the core primitive of transformer architectures — is implemented as a native tensor operation.




flowchart LR
    Q["Q [batch, seq, d_k]"] --> MM["Q · K^T"]
    K["K [batch, seq, d_k]"] --> MM
    MM --> SC["÷ sqrt(d_k)"] --> SM["Softmax"] --> MV["× V"]
    V["V [batch, seq, d_k]"] --> MV
    MV --> O["Output\n[batch, seq, d_k]"]


Scaled dot-product attention — softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V — as a single native CJC-Lang operation.



// Scaled dot-product attention: softmax(Q @ K^T / sqrt(d_k)) @ V
let Q = Tensor.randn([2, 8, 64]);   // [batch, seq_len, d_k]
let K = Tensor.randn([2, 8, 64]);
let V = Tensor.randn([2, 8, 64]);
let output = attention(Q, K, V);      // -> [2, 8, 64]
This computes softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V with batch matrix multiplication and deterministic softmax. It’s the core primitive you’d need to build transformer-style architectures.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Deep Network Stress Tests
Beyond the architecture-specific examples above, CJC-Lang includes benchmark programs that push forward passes to realistic scale:










Benchmark
Depth
Width
Batch
Verified




bench_nn_deep
50 layers
64
4
Bit-identical via snap_hash


bench_nn_wide
10 layers
256
16
Bit-identical via snap_hash



Both benchmarks insert snap_hash() checkpoints every few layers, producing cryptographic hashes of intermediate activations. Running twice with --seed 42 produces identical hashes at every checkpoint — proving that determinism holds through deep chains of matmul, bias, ReLU, and normalization.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
What This Proves
These aren’t theoretical capabilities. Each architecture has been implemented, run through both execution engines, and verified with assertions:

The REINFORCE agent plays chess (69 tests verify the full pipeline)
The PINN converges to the analytical solution of a differential equation
The CNN processes real 4D tensor shapes with convolution, pooling, and batch normalization
The attention mechanism handles batched multi-sequence inputs
The deep network benchmarks verify hash-level determinism through 50 sequential layers

All of them produce identical results across runs. In CJC-Lang, that’s not an opt-in mode — it’s how computation works.

If you haven’t read the introduction to CJC-Lang and the design decisions behind the language, start with Introducing CJC-Lang.
Next up: I’ll write about what it took to build a dual-engine compiler pipeline in Rust — and the design decisions that made parity testing between an AST interpreter and a register machine actually tractable.






Follow the project



CJC-Lang is open source and under active development. Follow @adamezzat for updates as new features land.

title: TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
text: 🕑 ~30 min read

title: TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
text:
The Problem
When I started building CJC-Lang, the core promise was simple: same seed, same output, always. Every part of the language enforces this — ordered collections, Kahan summation, seeded RNG, no FMA in SIMD kernels.
But then I needed to handle data. Filter a table. Group rows. Compute aggregates. Join two datasets.
The obvious move was to reach for an existing DataFrame library. But when I looked at what’s available, I kept running into the same problems:
Hash-based grouping. Most DataFrame libraries — including Pandas and Polars — use hash maps to group rows. Hash maps are fast, but their iteration order may depend on internal hash seeds or execution strategy. Depending on the configuration, grouping the same data twice can produce rows in a different order. That makes bit-identical reproducibility difficult to guarantee.
Floating-point reduction order. Call sum() on a million floats in Pandas. The result depends on the order of accumulation. Add the numbers in a different order and you get different rounding errors. Small differences, but they compound — and they mean “same data, different result” depending on implementation details. (Polars and other libraries may use more sophisticated reduction strategies, but the result can still vary across execution modes or platforms.)
Parallel scheduling. Libraries that use thread pools for parallel execution (Polars uses Rayon, for example) gain significant throughput. But thread scheduling is inherently non-deterministic, which means parallel aggregation can introduce ordering variation between runs — even with identical input. Whether this matters depends on the workload, but for bit-identical reproducibility it’s a concern.
Aliasing mutations. Pandas has an entire warning category (SettingWithCopyWarning) dedicated to the fact that modifying a filtered subset might or might not modify the original DataFrame. Whether it does depends on internal memory layout decisions that are invisible to the user.
None of these are bugs. They’re design trade-offs made by libraries that prioritize speed over reproducibility. But they break CJC-Lang’s core contract. So I built something else.

title: TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
text:
What TidyView Is
TidyView is a three-layer data manipulation system:








Layer
Type
What it does




Immutable view
TidyView
Zero-copy lens: bitmask + projection over shared data


Materialized frame
TidyFrame
Copy-on-write buffer for mutations


Grouped view
GroupedTidyView
Group index for aggregation



The API surface is modeled on R’s dplyr — filter, select, mutate, group_by, summarise, arrange, join. If you’ve used the tidyverse, you already know the vocabulary.
But the implementation borrows from a different tradition entirely: analytical databases.

How Data Flows Through a TidyView Pipeline





flowchart TD
    A["CSV / Data Source"] --> B["Base DataFrame<br/><i>immutable, Rc-shared</i>"]
    B --> C["TidyView<br/><i>zero-copy lens</i>"]
    C --> D["filter( )<br/><i>bitmask A — no data copied</i>"]
    D --> E["filter( )<br/><i>bitmask B — AND with A</i>"]
    E --> F["group_by( )<br/><i>BTreeMap index over visible rows</i>"]
    F --> G["summarise( )<br/><i>Kahan reduction per group</i>"]
    G --> H["Materialized Result<br/><i>TidyFrame — new data</i>"]

    style A fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#e8f4fd,stroke:#2196F3,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#e8f4fd,stroke:#2196F3
    style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#FF9800
    style E fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#FF9800
    style F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9C27B0
    style G fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9C27B0
    style H fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4CAF50,stroke-width:2px





Every step from the base DataFrame through the filters shares the same underlying data. New memory is only allocated for bitmasks (orange) and the final materialized result (green). The original data (blue) is never modified.

title: TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
text:
The Database Connection
I didn’t set out to build a database engine. But the constraints I was working under — determinism, auditability, memory safety — kept pushing me toward the same solutions that database engineers arrived at decades ago.

Columnar Storage
Data is stored column-major:
// Internally:
columns: [
    ("name",    Str(["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dave"])),
    ("age",     Int([30, 25, 35, 28])),
    ("revenue", Float([150.0, 200.0, 175.0, 225.0])),
]
Each column is a contiguous typed buffer. When you compute sum(revenue), the runtime scans a single Vec<f64> — no pointer chasing, no skipping over unrelated fields. This is the same storage model used by Apache Arrow, DuckDB, and Parquet.
The alternative — row-major storage — would scatter each revenue value across a row struct alongside name and age data. For a table with 100 columns, computing a single-column aggregate would touch 100x more memory than necessary.


Bitmask Filtering
This is the design choice that makes everything else work. When you filter a TidyView, no data is copied. Instead, a bitmask records which rows passed the predicate:
// Original data: 4 rows
base: ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dave"]  // ages: 30, 25, 35, 28

// After filter(age > 28):
mask: [1, 0, 1, 1]  // Alice (30), Carol (35), Dave (28 fails, hypothetical: > 28)
In practice the mask is packed into 64-bit words — one bit per row. A million-row filter costs 122 KB of bitmask memory. The original data stays exactly where it was, untouched.
Chain three filters together? The bitmasks are ANDed. Still no data copied. Still 122 KB.
let sales: Any = read_csv("sales.csv");
let df: Any = sales
    |> filter(col("region") == "West")
    |> filter(col("revenue") > 1000.0)
    |> filter(col("year") == 2025);
Each filter call returns a new TidyView with a new bitmask. All three views share the same underlying data. The CSV is loaded once. This is the same technique used by bitmap indexes in Oracle and selection vectors in DuckDB.


Predicate Pushdown
TidyView has a lazy evaluation layer where operations build a logical plan tree before executing. The optimizer rewrites this tree using three classic database passes:
Pass 1 — Filter merging. Consecutive filters are combined into a single filter with an AND predicate.
Pass 2 — Predicate pushdown. Filters are pushed as close to the data source as possible.
Pass 3 — Redundant select elimination. Select nodes that project all columns are removed.
Here’s why this matters. Suppose you write:
let result: Any = df
    |> mutate("bonus", col("salary") * 0.1)
    |> filter(col("department") == "Engineering");
Without optimization, this computes bonus for every row, then filters. With predicate pushdown, the optimizer recognizes that department == "Engineering" doesn’t depend on the bonus column, so it pushes the filter below the mutate. Now filtering happens first, and bonus is only computed for engineering rows.
The pushdown rules follow standard relational algebra:

Push past select — always safe
Push past arrange — filtering doesn’t affect sort order
Push past mutate — only if the predicate doesn’t reference a mutated column
Push into join branches — if the predicate references only one side
Never past group_by + summarise — aggregation changes row identity
Never past distinct — uniqueness changes row identity

These are the same rules taught in database systems textbooks. They apply here because TidyView’s operation set is relational algebra — just with tidyverse names.


Zone Maps
Each column tracks lightweight statistics:
// ColumnStats for the "revenue" column:
min: 150.0
max: 225.0
sorted_ascending: false
n_distinct: 4
When you write filter(col("revenue") > 500.0), the zone map says: max is 225, so no row can satisfy > 500. The entire filter can be skipped.
When a column is sorted, the system uses binary search instead of a linear scan — O(log N) instead of O(N).
These are the same optimizations that Vertica, Redshift, and Parquet use at the storage layer. In TidyView they operate at the column level because the entire “database” fits in memory.

title: TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
text:
What This Prevents
The architectural choices above aren’t just about performance. They prevent specific classes of bugs that plague traditional DataFrame workflows.

The Aliasing Problem
In Pandas:
df = pd.DataFrame({"x": [1, 2, 3], "y": [4, 5, 6]})
subset = df[df.x > 1]
subset["z"] = 99      # Does this modify df? Maybe.
Whether subset["z"] = 99 modifies the original df depends on whether Pandas chose to return a view or a copy when you wrote df[df.x > 1]. This is the SettingWithCopyWarning — one of the most common sources of confusion in data science code.
In TidyView, this problem doesn’t exist. The base data lives behind an Rc (reference-counted pointer) and is never modified. When you need to mutate, TidyFrame checks the reference count:

If only one reference exists, mutate in place
If multiple references exist, deep-clone first, then mutate the clone

The check is deterministic — it’s a simple integer comparison, not a heuristic. The other views are never affected.


The Order-Dependent Mutation Problem
In Pandas:
df = df.assign(a = df.x + 1, b = df.a * 2)
Does df.a in the second expression see the newly computed a or the original a? In Pandas, it sees the new one. The result depends on the order of the assignments. Swap a and b and you get different data.
In TidyView, mutate uses snapshot semantics: all column references are validated against the column list frozen before any assignments execute. Every expression sees the data as it was when the mutate call started:
let result: Any = df
    |> mutate("a", col("x") + 1)
    |> mutate("b", col("a") * 2);
// col("a") always reads the ORIGINAL "a" column
// If "a" didn't exist before, this is an error — not a silent dependency
This is the same principle as SQL’s UPDATE SET a = b, b = a — the SET clause reads from a snapshot, so this correctly swaps the values rather than setting both to b.


The Hidden Null Problem
Pandas DataFrames can have NaN or None in any column at any time. You discover this at runtime, usually when an aggregation silently skips missing values and gives you a number that’s subtly wrong.
In TidyView, the base Column type has no null representation:
// Column — always fully populated:
Int(Vec<i64>)
Float(Vec<f64>)
Str(Vec<String>)
Bool(Vec<bool>)
There’s no Option wrapper. No sentinel values. If you have a TidyView, its data is guaranteed null-free by type.
Nulls exist, but they’re quarantined in a separate type called NullCol. You only encounter NullCol through operations that can introduce nulls — left joins (unmatched rows), pivot_wider (missing combinations), full joins. These operations return a NullableFrame instead of a TidyFrame, and the type boundary makes the null-possibility explicit.
The question “does my data have nulls?” is answered by the type, not by scanning every cell.


The Floating-Point Drift Problem
# Pandas
pd.Series([1e16, 1.0, -1e16]).sum()  # → 0.0 (wrong)
The mathematically correct answer is 1.0. Pandas gives 0.0 because it uses naive left-to-right summation: 1e16 + 1.0 rounds to 1e16 (the 1.0 is lost to floating-point precision), then 1e16 - 1e16 = 0.0.
TidyView uses Kahan compensated summation for every floating-point reduction — sum, mean, variance, sd, all of them. Kahan summation tracks a correction term that captures the rounding error:
// Kahan summation:
//   sum = 0, c = 0
//   y = 1e16 - 0 = 1e16;    t = 0 + 1e16 = 1e16;    c = (1e16 - 1e16) - 1e16 = 0
//   y = 1.0 - 0 = 1.0;      t = 1e16 + 1.0 = 1e16;   c = (1e16 - 1e16) - 1.0 = -1.0
//   y = -1e16 - (-1.0);      ...compensates the lost 1.0
//   Result: 1.0 (correct)
Same input, same result, every time. Not approximately correct — exactly correct within the limits of double precision.

title: TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
section: How to Audit a TidyView Pipeline
text:
How to Audit a TidyView Pipeline
This is where the architecture pays off in practice. When something goes wrong in a data pipeline, the question is always: where did the data go wrong?
In TidyView, every intermediate step is inspectable because the original data is never modified. Every filter produces a bitmask you can examine. Every group_by produces a group index with key values and row indices. And the original data stays intact behind a shared reference, so you can always go back and check it.

Inspection Methods
At any point in a pipeline, you can call:
view.mask()           // → the bitmask — which rows are visible
view.proj()           // → the projection map — which columns are visible
grouped.group_index() // → group keys + row indices per group
From the mask:

mask.iter_set() gives the exact indices of visible rows
mask.count_ones() gives the count of surviving rows

From the projection:

proj.indices() gives which column indices are visible

The original data is always there behind the Rc<DataFrame> — no operation destroys it.


A Worked Example
Suppose you’re analyzing quarterly sales data and you get a result that looks wrong. In a traditional DataFrame library, you’d re-run the pipeline with print statements and hope to catch the problem. In TidyView, you can inspect every intermediate state because nothing was destroyed.
Here’s a concrete scenario. You have eight sales records and you want total revenue by product in the West region:
let sales = Csv.parse("product,region,revenue\nWidget,West,150\nGadget,West,200\nWidget,East,175\nGadget,East,225\nWidget,West,300\nGizmo,East,50\nGadget,West,180\nGizmo,East,75\n");
let view = sales.view();

let west = view.filter(dexpr_binop("==", col("region"), "West"));
let grouped = west.group_by(["product"]);
let summary = grouped.summarise("total_rev", tidy_sum("revenue"));
The summary shows Widget: 450, Gadget: 380. Is that right? Let’s audit backward.
Step 1: Which rows survived the filter?
The bitmask after filter(region == "West") has bits set at positions [0, 1, 4, 6]. That’s four rows out of eight. You can verify: row 0 is Widget/West/150, row 1 is Gadget/West/200, row 4 is Widget/West/300, row 6 is Gadget/West/180. All West. None of the East rows slipped through.
Step 2: How did the groups form?
The group index shows two groups in first-occurrence order:

Group “Widget” → base-frame rows [0, 4]
Group “Gadget” → base-frame rows [1, 6]

Notice: Gizmo isn’t here. Both Gizmo rows were East, so they were filtered out before grouping. If you expected Gizmo to appear, the bitmask tells you exactly why it didn’t — rows 5 and 7 have their bits cleared.
Step 3: Verify the aggregation
Widget revenue: row 0 (150) + row 4 (300) = 450. Gadget revenue: row 1 (200) + row 6 (180) = 380. Both use Kahan compensated summation, so even with millions of rows the result would be bit-identical across runs.
Step 4: Is the original data intact?
Yes. view.nrows() still returns 8. You can re-filter, re-group, or inspect any column at any time. If the final number is wrong, you trace backward through bitmasks and group indices until you find where your understanding diverged from reality.
This is fundamentally different from a system where df = df[df.region == "West"] discards the filtered-out rows. In that system, if the filter was wrong, the evidence is gone. In TidyView, the evidence is the bitmask, and the original data is still there to check against.


Catching Mistakes at Compile Time
TidyView’s type system and error reporting catch common mistakes before they become wrong numbers:
Typo in a column name:
view.filter(dexpr_binop(">", col("revnue"), 100));
// → Error: column `revnue` not found
Self-referencing mutate:
view.mutate("bonus", dexpr_binop("*", col("salary"), 0.1))
    .mutate("double_bonus", dexpr_binop("*", col("bonus"), 2));
The second mutate sees bonus because it’s a separate call on the result of the first. But if you tried to reference a column in the same mutate call that creates it:
view.mutate("a", dexpr_binop("+", col("x"), 1),
            "b", dexpr_binop("*", col("a"), 2));
// → Error: column `a` not found
This is snapshot semantics — all column references are validated against the column list frozen before any assignments. You can’t accidentally create an order-dependent pipeline within a single operation.
Type mismatch:
view.filter(dexpr_binop("+", col("name"), 1));
// → Error: type mismatch — expected Bool predicate, got Int
The filter predicate must evaluate to Bool. Arithmetic on a string column, or a predicate that returns a number instead of true/false, is caught immediately with an error that names the exact types involved.


Error Messages That Point to the Problem
When something goes wrong, TidyView’s error types name the exact issue:
ColumnNotFound("revnue")       // Typo — you meant "revenue"
DuplicateColumn("total")       // Two columns with the same name in select
PredicateNotBool { got: "Int" } // filter predicate returned Int, not Bool
TypeMismatch { expected: "Float", got: "Str" }  // arithmetic on a string column
LengthMismatch { expected: 1000, got: 500 }     // broadcast size mismatch
EmptyGroup                     // first() or last() called on a group with zero rows
CapacityExceeded { limit: 65535, got: 70000 }   // too many distinct levels for a factor column
Every error variant names the specific column, type, or value that caused the problem. You know what went wrong and where, not just that something failed.
EmptyGroup catches a subtle bug: if an aggressive filter removes all rows from a group and you then call first() or last() in your summarise, you get a clear error instead of a silent null or a crash. CapacityExceeded guards the factor column type — CJC-Lang’s factor columns use u16 codes (max 65,535 distinct levels) for cache efficiency, and this error tells you exactly when your data exceeds that budget.

title: TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
text:
Why Not Just Use Polars?
Polars is a good library. It’s fast, it has a lazy API, and it uses Arrow-backed columnar storage. If I were building a Python application, I’d probably use it.
But Polars makes different trade-offs than TidyView:









Polars
TidyView




Grouping
Hash-based (fast; order may vary by execution mode)
BTreeMap (deterministic first-occurrence order)


Summation
Optimized reduction (fast; may vary across platforms)
Kahan compensated (deterministic, bit-identical)


Parallelism
Rayon thread pool (high throughput)
Single-threaded (deterministic, no scheduling variance)


Null model
Nullable everywhere (Arrow validity bitmaps)
Null-free by default, NullCol only where needed


Sampling
System RNG
Seeded LCG-based Fisher-Yates



None of Polars’ trade-offs are wrong — and Polars is significantly faster for most workloads. It’s a production-grade library optimized for throughput. TidyView is optimizing for a different property: reproducibility — the guarantee that the same pipeline on the same data always produces the same result, bit for bit, across repeated runs. (All benchmarks and determinism tests were run on Windows; cross-platform verification is ongoing.) TidyView will be slower than Polars on almost any benchmark. That’s the expected cost of determinism.
When your work requires an audit trail — when a regulator, a reviewer, or future-you needs to verify that a result is correct — that guarantee can matter more than raw speed.

title: TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
text:
Benchmark Results
The claims above — bitmask filtering is cheap, chained filters don’t copy data, determinism holds across runs — are testable. I wrote a 38-test benchmark suite that measures TidyView’s performance and verifies its architectural guarantees. Here are the results, run on my machine (Intel i7-11390H, Windows, Rust 1.91 release mode, 100K-row datasets).






A note on context. These benchmarks measure TidyView against itself — lazy vs. eager, scaling behavior, determinism proofs. They are not comparative benchmarks against Polars, DuckDB, or Pandas. TidyView is single-threaded and prioritizes determinism over throughput; it will be slower than production-optimized libraries on raw performance. The goal here is to verify that TidyView’s architectural claims hold, not to claim speed parity with tools that make different trade-offs.




A. Filter Microbenchmarks



Test
Lazy (µs)
Eager (µs)
Ratio




Int filter, 100K rows
165
11,943
72×


Float filter, 100K rows
191
11,804
62×


String filter, 100K rows
352
6,255
18×


Int filter, 1M rows
2,002
157,135
79×



What this proves: Lazy bitmask filtering is 62–79× faster than eager materialization for numeric columns. The filter itself just scans one column and sets bits — no row copying, no allocation beyond the bitmask.
String filters benefit from a columnar fast path that evaluates the predicate directly against the column buffer (bitwise, not row-by-row) — bringing them to 352 µs, an 18× lazy/eager ratio.
The 1M-row int filter takes about 2 ms to produce a bitmask. Materializing — actually copying the surviving rows into a new DataFrame — takes 157 ms. If you’re chaining multiple filters, you save that materialization cost at every step.


B. Chained Filter Composition



Test
Time (µs)




Single int filter, 100K
220


Two chained int filters, 100K
384


Int + string chain, 100K
609


Two string filters, 100K
813



What this proves: Chaining two filters costs roughly the sum of the individual filters, not more. The second filter reads through the first filter’s bitmask — it only examines rows where the bit is set. No intermediate DataFrame is created. The cost of the chain is dominated by the most expensive predicate type (string comparison).


C. Group-By + Summarise



Test
Groups
Time (µs)




Count, 4 groups, 100K
4
3,099


Mixed agg (5 stats), 4 groups, 100K
4
5,918


Count + Sum, 100 groups, 100K
100
10,734


Count, 10K groups, 100K
10,000
42,778


Count + Sum + Mean, 10K groups, 100K
10,000
39,782



What this proves: Group-by cost scales with the number of distinct groups, not just row count. With 4 groups (the region column), grouping 100K rows takes about 3 ms. With 10K groups, it takes 40 ms. This is the cost of the BTreeMap-based index — O(N log G) where G is the number of groups. Adding more aggregation functions adds relatively little overhead; the dominant cost is building the group index, not computing the aggregates.
The single-key fast path (used when grouping by one column) avoids per-row heap allocation entirely: the BTreeMap key is a borrowed reference into the column data, not a cloned value. For the 4-group case, this means 100K lookups with zero string copies.


D. Sorting



Test
Time (µs)




Float descending, 100K
1,378


Int ascending, 100K
1,201


String ascending, 100K
18,343


Multi-key (string + float), 100K
41,638



What this proves: Numeric sorts on 100K rows complete in about 1.2–1.4 ms thanks to pre-resolved column references — the sort comparator holds a direct &Column pointer instead of doing a name lookup on every comparison. String sorting is roughly 13× more expensive than numeric sorting due to comparison cost. Multi-key sorting adds the cost of a secondary comparator.
All sorts use Rust’s stable sort (sort_by), which preserves the relative order of equal elements — important for determinism. The result is stored as a lazy permutation vector, not a new DataFrame. Operations like select, rename, and relocate propagate the permutation without resolving it.


E. Joins



Test
Time (µs)




Inner join, 100K × 100
33,936


Inner join, 100K × 1K
40,507


Left join, 100K × 100
38,712



What this proves: Join performance is dominated by the left table size. Increasing the right table from 100 to 1,000 rows adds about 20% to the join time, because the right table is indexed and lookups are O(log K) where K is the number of unique keys.
Both the right-side index build and the left-side probe use borrowed keys — ColumnKeyRef references directly into the column data without cloning. For single-key joins (the common case), the BTreeMap key is a single borrowed value with no Vec wrapper, eliminating per-row allocation on both sides.


F. Full Pipeline Benchmarks



Pipeline
Time (µs)




filter → group → summarise → arrange, 100K
4,151


filter → mutate → group → summarise, 100K
40,640


join → filter → group → summarise, 100K
97,769



What this proves: Realistic multi-step pipelines compose predictably. The filter-group-summarise-arrange pipeline (4.2 ms) is faster than the sum of its parts because the filter reduces the number of rows that downstream operations process, and the lazy sort avoids materializing a new DataFrame. The join-based pipeline is slowest because joins are the most expensive operation and produce a new materialized DataFrame.


G. Scaling Behavior
Row scaling (filter):



Rows

Filter time scales linearly with row count — exactly what you’d expect from a single-pass scan. 10× more rows ≈ 10× more time.

Materialization scales linearly with column count because it has to copy every column. This is the columnar storage payoff: a table with 101 columns filters as fast as a table with 3 columns.
Group cardinality scaling (100K rows):
